AST-2.measurement_goal: Measure coverage of retention policies across all data types (percentage of data assets with an assigned retention period), the percentage/count of data disposed of in accordance with policy, and the time between retention expiration and actual deletion (mean and distribution) to prove retention procedures are applied and enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AST-2 is primarily preventive (establishing procedures and coverage), so percentage metrics (coverage, percent disposed per policy) directly show policy adherence; counts and rates (deletions, overdue items) and trends (improvement over time) add operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of data assets/records with an assigned retention classification and retention expiration date; number and percentage of assets deleted within the policy timeframe; deletion job execution timestamps and asset identifiers to compute time-to-delete (deletion_timestamp - retention_expiration_date); records of retention exceptions or retention-extension approvals. A usable metric: percentage of records deleted within X days of retention expiry, by data classification.
data_signals: data inventory rows with data type, data owner, and assigned retention_period
retention policy assignment timestamps (when policy was applied to an asset)
automated deletion job execution logs (job_id, asset_id, deletion_timestamp, deletion_status)
retention_expiration_date field per asset/record
time difference between retention_expiration_date and deletion_timestamp
count/percentage of assets flagged as 'disposed_of_according_to_policy' or 'overdue_for_deletion'
secure storage metadata (storage_location, encryption_status, archival_state)
retention exception/approval records (request_id, approver, justification, extension_period)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated lifecycle management logs showing deletion_timestamp and asset_id for each expired asset, full data inventory mapping to retention_period, and audit trail for retention exceptions.
Weak: Manual spreadsheets or one-off deletion screenshots, infrequent ad-hoc attestations, or deletion reports without asset identifiers or timestamps.
Strong: Cryptographic or irreversible wipe confirmation or deletion_status codes in logs; Weak: verbal confirmations or unsigned change requests.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control centers on establishing procedures, policies, and automated lifecycle management to prevent retention-related risk (phrases: 'procedures define the duration', 'establishing robust data retention procedures', 'automated tools for data lifecycle management').

AST-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires lifecycle and deletion-timing metrics (retention assignment coverage and time-to-delete) focused on disposal and policy adherence, rather than broader asset inventory or access controls covered by sibling asset-management controls.

AST-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of production assets (hardware, software, data) that are recorded with required fields (owner, location, configuration, lifecycle status), the frequency of inventory audits, and the median time to reconcile inventory discrepancies and remove/decommission assets after termination or retirement.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: AST-3 is primarily preventive — auditors need coverage measurements (e.g., percent of assets accurately recorded). Counts and trends are useful secondary metrics to show raw inventory size, discovery of unmanaged assets, and remediation over time; rates measure reconciliation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of production assets in the centralized inventory that have required attributes (asset_id, asset_type, owner, location, configuration/hash, lifecycle_status, tag) compared to discovered assets from discovery scans; record timestamps of inventory updates and audit events and measure median time from discrepancy detection to reconciliation and time from employee termination to asset deprovisioning.
data_signals: centralized inventory records with fields: asset_id, asset_type, owner, location, configuration snapshot, lifecycle_status, asset_tag
automated asset discovery/scan results (IP, MAC, hostname, software installed) with timestamps
inventory change logs / audit trail (who changed, what attribute, timestamp)
inventory audit reports (audit date, discrepancies found, reconciled count, reconciliation timestamps)
onboarding/termination deprovisioning logs showing asset return/removal timestamps
integration/sync logs between inventory system and security tools (CMDB sync, endpoint management sync) indicating coverage
unmanaged or unknown asset count (assets discovered but not in inventory) and overdue reconciliation count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated discovery logs matched to inventory records and regular audit reports showing reconcilation timestamps and ownership fields populated
Strong: integration sync logs proving near-real-time updates between discovery tools and the centralized inventory
Weak: annual manual spreadsheets or one-off audit snapshots without change logs or reconciliation timestamps
Weak: inventory entries missing owner, configuration, or lifecycle status even if asset names exist

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing and maintaining an inventory system and ongoing tracking to prevent unauthorized or unmanaged assets — language like 'ensure that all production assets... are accurately tracked and managed' indicates preventive control.

AST-3.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on complete, attribute-level inventory and lifecycle reconciliation (coverage, ownership, and reconciliation time), whereas sibling controls would measure detection, access, or remediation; mapping to provided focus_areas is the closest fit since there is no explicit 'asset_management' focus_area in the allowed list.

BCD-1.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and currency of the CDR program (BIA completion, inventory of critical assets, documented RTO/RPO per asset), the frequency and success rate of recovery tests and simulations, and actual recovery performance (time to recover and data recovered) versus defined RTO/RPO targets and training completion rates for responsible personnel.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires demonstrating coverage and compliance (e.g., percent of critical assets covered, percent of tests passing, percent of personnel trained), while counts (number of tests, incidents) and trends over time show ongoing effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Quantify CDR program coverage and effectiveness: percent of critical systems/assets with BIA completed and RTO/RPO defined; backup success rate and percent of successful recovery tests; mean time to recover (MTTR) for test exercises and actual incidents compared to RTO; frequency of plan tests and percent of staff with up-to-date training. Include timestamps and pass/fail outcomes to enable SLA comparison and trending.
data_signals: Business Impact Analysis (BIA) document status and completion dates
inventory of critical assets with assigned RTOs and RPOs
backup job logs (timestamps, success/failure, size, retention) and restore execution logs
recovery test execution records (test start/end timestamps, pass/fail, scope, test runbook used)
incident recovery timelines and post-incident reports (actual recovery time vs defined RTO)
frequency and dates of plan testing and simulation exercises
training completion records for personnel with CDR roles (attendance, date, curriculum)
CDR plan revision history and approval timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated backup and restore logs with timestamps and verified restore results, regular scheduled test records showing pass/fail and measured recovery times, documented BIA and asset RTO/RPO mappings, and up-to-date training records.
Weak: a single undated or unsigned plan document, one-off manual test screenshots, anecdotal claims of recovery without logs, or no measured RTO/RPO comparisons.
Strong: recurring simulation results demonstrating MTTR consistently within RTOs and documented post-test remediation tracked to closure.
Weak: only policy text saying RTO/RPO exist without measurable test or incident evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing a CDR plan, performing BIAs, defining RTO/RPO and training—activities that prevent or limit impact by preparing procedures and roles.

BCD-1.differentiation_note: This control is focused on establishing and validating a documented CDR program (BIA, RTO/RPO, backups, tests, training) — unlike operational incident detection metrics, it emphasizes program coverage, test frequency/results, and recovery performance versus targets.

BCD-2.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required BC/DR tests completed on schedule and the proportion of systems that met defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) per test; also report count of post-test remediation items and average time-to-remediate those items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires coverage and adherence to defined objectives (e.g., tests conducted, RTO/RPO met) — percentage metrics (coverage, SLA attainment) most directly show compliance; counts of tests and trending of RTO/RPO over time are useful secondary measures to show frequency and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of BC/DR tests executed by type (tabletop, simulation, full-scale) and date, percent of scheduled tests completed within the policy timeframe, per-system RTO and RPO achieved for each test, list and status of post-test remediation items with remediation timestamps and owners, and the inventory of systems/applications included in each test with criticality tier.
data_signals: BC/DR test execution records with test type (tabletop/simulation/full-scale) and timestamp
RTO achieved per system/application per test (measured time-to-restore)
RPO achieved per system/application per test (measured recovery point timestamp differences)
Scheduled vs. completed test log (dates and exceptions)
Post-test action item list with creation date, owner, remediation status, and remediation completion timestamp
System/application inventory included in each test with criticality classification
Test attendance/role participation logs and signed test reports
BC/DR plan version history and test plan documents
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped test execution logs with measured RTO/RPO values per system, signed test reports, and closed remediation items with timestamps and owners.
Strong: consistent completion of required tests (e.g., annual) with trend showing RTO/RPO improvements and timely closure of action items.
Weak: single point-in-time/manual summary (e.g., one post-mortem document) or anecdotal statements without measured RTO/RPO, attendance logs, or remediation timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control mandates having a BC/DR plan and regular testing to ensure capability to restore operations (emphasis on preparedness and coverage), which is primarily preventive though testing also provides detective assurance.

BCD-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurable BC/DR test execution and RTO/RPO attainment metrics (frequency, per-system recovery performance, and remediation closure), rather than merely documenting the existence of a plan or policy.

BCD-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of documented cyber risks that are covered by an active cybersecurity insurance policy, plus count and value of incident claims filed and the average time from incident to insurer notification and claim resolution to demonstrate coverage adequacy and operational readiness.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive / coverage control — auditors need a coverage percentage (what portion of identified risks/assets are insured). Counts of claims and trending of time-to-notify/resolve are useful secondary measures to show operational performance and changes over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percent of items in the organization's risk register (or asset/risk mapping) that have corresponding, active insurance coverage documented (policy ID, limits, deductibles). 2) For incident outcomes: count of incidents in period with claims filed, total claim payout amounts, and average time from incident date to claim submission and from submission to claim resolution. 3) Frequency and date of policy review/updates against the latest risk assessment.
data_signals: risk register entries with mapping to policy ID
insurance policy documents (policy number, effective/expiry dates, coverage limits, deductibles, exclusions)
policy review/update logs or meeting minutes
claims ledger or claim management records (claim ID, incident ID, submission timestamp, settlement timestamp, payout amount, denial/reason)
incident records indicating notification to insurer (notification timestamp, contact used)
risk assessment outputs showing identified cyber threats and estimated financial impact
policy renewal and insurer reputation / underwriter change records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed, current insurance policy documents plus a risk-to-policy coverage matrix and automated claim records with timestamps and settlement amounts; regular documented policy review meeting notes showing updates aligned to recent risk assessments.
Weak: verbal confirmation of coverage, expired policies, single-year/one-off policy documents, or absence of claim timestamps and settlement details.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states this is a "preventive control" that provides financial protection and requires policy selection and periodic updates.

BCD-3.differentiation_note: Unlike technical detection or remediation controls, this control requires measurement of contractual coverage and claims handling (policy documents, coverage mapping, claim timelines) rather than system telemetry or vulnerability counts.

BCD-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of database replication events that complete successfully within defined SLA windows, the average and distribution of replication lag per replica, the count and rate of replication errors/failures, and the pass rate and time-to-failover for periodic replication/failover tests.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive and specifies SLAs and replication success rates, so a percentage (successful events / total events within SLA) directly evidences control effectiveness; counts of errors and trending of lag over time are useful secondary signals to show operational health and improvement/degeneration.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of replication jobs that completed successfully within the SLA window per database cluster and per replica; (2) average and percentile replication lag (seconds) per replica and per replication topology; (3) count and rate of replication errors/failures grouped by error code and root cause; (4) failover test results with time-to-failover and pass/fail status. Include replication configuration/version snapshots to show consistent secure configuration across primary and replicas.
data_signals: replication job execution timestamps (start, end) per database/replica
last successful replication timestamp by replica
replication lag metric (seconds or ms) sampled over time per replica
replication error logs and error codes with counts and timestamps
successful versus failed replication event counts (per period) and SLA flag
failover/drill test results (timestamp, pass/fail, time-to-failover)
replication configuration snapshots / topology mapping (primary vs replica locations)
network throughput/bandwidth utilization for replication windows
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated replication logs and time-series lag metrics with alert history are strong evidence
Regular, documented failover test results and current replication configuration snapshots increase evidence strength
Point-in-time screenshots or occasional manual copies without logs are weak evidence
Rising replication lag trends, repeated error codes, or unresolved failed replication events indicate weak or failing control evidence

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' that 'ensure[s] availability and integrity' by creating and maintaining copies and defining replication frequency and failover procedures.

BCD-4.differentiation_note: This control is specific to replication mechanics and operational monitoring (lag, replication success rates, failover tests) rather than general backup or recovery policies—its unique measurement need is continuous replication health metrics and SLA-based success percentages rather than single-point backup evidences.

BCD-5.measurement_goal: Measure backup job success and coverage for all production assets (percentage of scheduled backups completed), the restore-test success rate and measured time-to-recover (RTO) during recovery tests, and encryption/compliance status of backup data to demonstrate recoverability and integrity.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about ability to recover (restorative performance) so rates (restore success rate, backup completion rate, restore success per test) are most useful; percentages and counts support coverage and volume measurements and trends show ongoing reliability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled backup jobs vs successful completions per backup target (machine, database, bucket) over the audit period; percentage of production assets with a successful backup within configured recovery window; number and percentage of successful restore tests and measured time-to-restore per test; encryption-in-transit and at-rest status per backup artifact; backup retention compliance versus policy.
data_signals: backup job execution logs with timestamps and status codes (success/failure)
scheduled backup job definitions and frequency from backup system
last successful backup timestamp per production asset
inventory of production assets mapped to backup coverage (asset id -> backup target)
restore test records: test start/complete timestamps, data restored, success/failure, measured RTO
backup artifact metadata: encryption-in-transit flag, encryption-at-rest flag, retention period
audit logs documenting backup activities and recovery tests
backup failure/error messages and failure reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated backup job logs and per-asset last-success timestamps (strong)
Documented, repeatable restore test results with measured RTO and data integrity verification (strong)
Presence of encryption flags on backup artifacts and automated retention enforcement (strong)
Ad-hoc manual export or one-off restore screenshots without logs or per-asset coverage data (weak)
Annual/manual backup reports alone without recent automated job logs or restore tests (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description emphasizes restoring operations and conducting recovery tests (e.g., 'restore operations swiftly', 'recovery procedures', 'frequency of successful restorations'), which aligns with corrective/recovery controls.

BCD-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets backup execution, coverage, restore-test success and measured RTO/RPO for production data (recoverability), unlike sibling controls that might focus on access, encryption policy, or configuration hardening; measurements must include per-asset backup/restore metrics and integrity/encryption flags.

BCD-6.measurement_goal: Show the percentage of production-critical services provisioned across multiple AZs (meeting 'full workload' capability per zone), the failover success rate for scheduled and unscheduled events, and measured recovery characteristics (RTO/RPO and response-time impact) during failovers. Also report data replication health (lag/success) and uptime percentages by service to demonstrate continuous multi-AZ resilience.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and configuration (multi-AZ coverage and successful replication), so percentage metrics (coverage, uptime %) are primary; rates and counts are useful secondary measures for failover success frequency and test counts, and trend shows ongoing effectiveness over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of production services (by criticality tier) that have active resources provisioned in two or more AZs. 2) Failover test records: timestamped test executions with pass/fail, RTO/RPO measured, and response-time delta during failover. 3) Continuous availability metrics: per-service uptime percentage and error-rate during failovers. 4) Data replication metrics: replication lag (ms/seconds), last successful replication timestamp, and replication error counts.
data_signals: infrastructure provisioning records showing AZ assignments for each production service or instance
asset/service inventory with criticality tier and AZ distribution
load balancer configuration and distribution logs (target lists, traffic distribution percentages, health-check results)
failover test execution logs with timestamps, pass/fail outcomes, RTO/RPO measurements, and test run owners
monitoring/metric time series: per-service uptime percentage, response time during normal and failover windows
data replication logs/metrics (replication lag, last successful replication timestamp, replication error/failure count)
incident and outage logs with root-cause linking to AZ failure, outage duration, and remediation actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped provisioning records plus continuous monitoring time series (uptime, response-time, replication lag) and structured failover test logs showing repeated successful tests.
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots or one-off manual statements of configuration, or annual test reports without recorded RTO/RPO and monitoring data.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is described as 'a preventive control designed to enhance the resilience and availability' and focuses on provisioning and configuration to avoid single points of failure.

BCD-6.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of AZ-level provisioning, load-balancer distribution, replication health, and structured failover test results — unlike other continuity controls that may focus on backup retention or runbooks, BCD-6 requires live multi-AZ deployment and failover performance evidence.

CAP-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of critical systems whose CPU, memory, storage and network utilization remain within defined capacity thresholds, the count of scheduled capacity reviews completed on time, and trending of key utilization metrics and forecasted capacity vs. actual usage across planning windows.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and threshold adherence; percentage metrics (e.g., percent of systems within capacity thresholds) directly show compliance with capacity policy, while trends and counts show ongoing monitoring and review frequency required by the control.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each critical system or asset tier: time-series utilization metrics (CPU %, memory %, disk % or GB free, network bandwidth %/Mbps), timestamps of load-testing runs and their results (throughput, error rate), scheduled capacity review records with sign-offs and dates, and capacity forecast projections. Aggregate these into: percent of assets within policy thresholds, number of reviews completed on schedule, open capacity issues, and variance between forecasted and actual usage.
data_signals: CPU utilization time-series per host (timestamped % values)
memory usage time-series per host (timestamped % values)
disk/storage utilization and available GB per volume (timestamped)
network bandwidth utilization per interface or instance (timestamped Mbps or %)
load test execution records (timestamp, requests/sec, errors, latency)
capacity forecast documents or model outputs (timestamp, projected usage by period)
capacity review artifacts and sign-off logs (review date, reviewer, decisions)
capacity threshold/ policy definitions for asset tiers (threshold values by metric)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous time-series monitoring with host identifiers, threshold definitions, dated capacity review reports with sign-offs, and repeatable load-test artifacts.
Strong: consistent forecasting outputs with historical accuracy metrics (forecast vs actual).
Weak: one-off manual notes or a single annual report without time-series utilization data or documented thresholds.
Weak: unscheduled ad-hoc load tests lacking timestamps or test parameters.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames the control as a proactive, recurring assessment and planning activity ('regularly assesses', 'proactive measure'), which is characteristic of preventive controls.

CAP-1.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on performance/capacity metrics and forecasting (CPU/memory/storage/network and load-test evidence) rather than access, vulnerability, or incident detection — measurement must emphasize utilization time-series, forecasts, and documented review sign-offs rather than logs or vulnerability counts.

CFG-1.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of implemented changes that followed the documented change management process (requested, reviewed, approved, documented) and the counts of unauthorized or emergency rollbacks; measure CAB review coverage and the percentage of changes with an approved rollback plan to demonstrate control over infrastructure, software, and data changes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/adherence metrics (percentage of changes following the process). Counts of changes and trending of adherence over time supplement percentage coverage to show volume and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute (a) total number of change requests and number implemented, (b) percentage of implemented changes that have an associated approved change request and CAB approval where required, (c) count and percentage of changes with documented rollback plans, (d) count of unauthorized changes or changes that caused incidents, and (e) configuration baseline versioning and change timestamps to show traceability.
data_signals: change request records (requester, request_timestamp, change_id, change_description)
approval records and CAB meeting minutes (approver_id, approval_timestamp, CAB_decision)
change implementation logs (change_id, implementation_timestamp, implementer_id, success/failure, rollback_executed_flag)
rollback execution logs and rollback plan document links
automated change-tracking/audit tool logs showing config diffs and baseline version IDs
incident/ticket records referencing change_id (incident_id, incident_timestamp, impact_description)
change classification/type (standard/normal/emergency) and associated policy exception flags
results from periodic change-management audit/review reports (findings, remediation actions, audit_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous automated change-tracking logs with cryptographic timestamps, linked approval records (CAB minutes or electronic approvals), baseline snapshots and clear linkage from change_id→implementation→rollback→incident.
Weak: Point-in-time screenshots, manual spreadsheets, or ad-hoc email approvals without machine-readable audit trails; absence of CAB documentation or missing rollback plan links weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control requires establishing and enforcing a documented change process (requests, review, approval, rollback), which is preventive in nature.

CFG-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on establishing the CMS, documented policies, CAB reviews and rollback planning (governance and traceability), rather than purely technical configuration hardening or vulnerability scanning; its measurements must emphasize process adherence and approval traceability rather than vulnerability counts.

CHG-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of changes processed through the formal change workflow (change request created, CAB approval, pre-deployment testing, documented outcome) and track rework/rollback and time-to-approval/implementation to demonstrate adherence to the change management policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage/compliance percentages (e.g., percent of changes with CAB approval, percent tested), while counts and rates (change volume, emergency change rate) and trends (improvement over time) provide supporting context for SLAs and rework.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of change requests that include a completed change request form, documented impact assessment and rationale, CAB approval record, pre-deployment test evidence, implementation timestamp and outcome, plus counts of rework/rollback and mean time to approval and implementation. Include completeness checks for required documentation fields and emergency/unauthorized change counts.
data_signals: change request records with unique ID, requester, creation timestamp, planned implementation timestamp, and status
CAB approval records/meeting minutes linked to change IDs (approver, approval timestamp, decision)
pre-deployment testing artifacts linked to change ID (test results, test environment identifiers)
implementation execution logs with start and completion timestamps and outcome (success/failure/rollback)
change documentation fields presence: rationale, impact assessment, outcomes
rework/rollback flags and root-cause notes per change
counts of emergency or unauthorized changes and associated justifications
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Automated, ticketed change records with immutable timestamps, linked CAB approvals and stored test artifacts for each change; low emergency/unauthorized change rate; high documentation completeness and low rework rate.
Weak: Manual spreadsheets, ad-hoc email approvals, missing pre-deployment test evidence, or one-off screenshots of approvals; high proportion of undocumented or emergency changes.

affinity_keywords: percentage of changes that require rework
time taken for changes to be approved and implemented

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces formal policies, CAB approvals, pre-deployment testing and documentation to prevent unauthorized or risky changes (phrases: 'change advisory board (CAB)', 'testing environment', 'approval').

CHG-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measuring adherence to the formal change lifecycle (request → CAB approval → test → implement → review) and documentation completeness and rework rates, unlike sibling controls that might focus on access, patching, or incident detection.

CHG-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of production deployments and production-capable identities that are performed by authorized personnel with approved access (RBAC), MFA enabled, and recorded approval evidence; and count unauthorized or failed production access/deployment attempts and overdue access review items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control the most useful primary metric is coverage/adherence (percentage of production identities and deployments following RBAC/MFA/approval). Counts and rates supplement by showing absolute unauthorized attempts and frequency/trends of deviations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) percentage of production-capable accounts with RBAC roles aligned to least-privilege and MFA enabled; (b) percentage of production deployments where the deployer identity matches an authorized role and an approval record exists; (c) count and rate of unauthorized or failed production access/deployment attempts; and (d) number/percentage of production access review items completed on schedule.
data_signals: production deployment audit logs (timestamp, deployer identity, CI/CD job id, deployment target)
RBAC role definitions and permission mappings for production environments
user inventory of accounts with production access and their MFA enrollment status
access request and approval records (requestor, approver, timestamps, approval decision)
periodic access review records/attestations with reviewer and completion timestamp
authentication/authorization failure logs for production resources (failed MFA, denied API calls)
privileged account list and ownership/justification metadata
deployment pipeline run records linked to approver/approval ticket IDs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous, machine-readable deployment logs that link deployer identity to an approval ticket and RBAC role; system-enforced MFA flags and automated access-review attestations.
Weak: Point-in-time screenshots of access lists, manual spreadsheets of users, or one-off attestations without machine-logged deployment linkage.
Strong: Near-real-time alerts for denied or unauthorized deployment attempts vs. Weak: reliance only on periodic manual audits.

affinity_keywords: inventory of all users with production access

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' and details limiting access via RBAC, MFA, and approval workflows to prevent unauthorized production changes.

CHG-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets restriction and approval of production deployments (mapping deployer identity to approvals and RBAC/MFA state), unlike sibling change-management controls that may focus on versioning or testing — measurement must link deployment records to authorization artifacts rather than only change tickets.

CHG-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of active projects that follow the mandated SDLC artifacts and DevSecOps gates (planning, version control, automated testing, deployment approval) and track counts of unauthorized changes, production vulnerabilities introduced by development, and time-to-resolution for development-identified issues.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control needs coverage/ compliance measurements (percent of projects with required SDLC artifacts, percent of repos with automated tests) as the primary evidence; counts of exceptions and trending over time validate reductions in unauthorized changes and production vulnerabilities.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of projects/repos that have required SDLC artifacts (project plan, documented requirements, test plans) and DevSecOps gates (version control, PR reviews, automated test & SAST results) in the release pipeline; count of unauthorized change exceptions and production vulnerabilities attributed to recent code changes; mean time to remediate development-origin vulnerabilities and percent of developers completing secure-coding training.
data_signals: project SDLC documentation presence (timestamped artifact records)
version control repository existence and commit history per project
pull request / code review approval timestamps and approver identities
automated test pipeline execution reports with pass/fail and coverage metrics
SAST/DAST scan results keyed by project, vulnerability severity (e.g., CVSS/CWE), and timestamps
change approval records and documented unauthorized change/exception tickets
production incident / vulnerability records linked to a commit or release
secure coding training completion records by developer and date
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated CI/CD pipeline logs, SAST scan outputs, version control commit history and PR approvals, and linked change-approval records showing continuous enforcement.
Strong: automated linkage between vulnerabilities and originating commits plus remediation timestamps.
Weak: one-off manual attestations, undated or non-machine-readable checklists, or annual audit reports without pipeline/scan logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Control mandates establishing and documenting SDLC and DevSecOps practices to prevent unauthorized changes and vulnerabilities, indicating a preventive control.

CHG-3.differentiation_note: This control focuses on measuring SDLC coverage, DevSecOps gate enforcement, and secure-development training (coverage/percentage and linked pipeline evidence) rather than purely change-approval counts or incident response metrics used by sibling change controls.

CPL-1.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of the organization’s system description: percentage of system components and services documented, count of documentation updates tied to system changes, and average time between a system change and the corresponding update to the system description.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventative/documentary so coverage and timeliness are best expressed as percentages (percent of assets documented, percent of changes with updates). Counts (change events, missing docs) and trend metrics (time-to-update over periods) supplement coverage to show operational behavior and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (a) percent of inventoryed system components (by environment and criticality) that have an associated system description artifact (architecture diagram, data flow map, controls list); (b) count of change requests that required documentation updates and the count completed; and (c) mean time from change request approval to documentation update (in days). Also capture review/audit findings that identify documentation discrepancies.
data_signals: canonical asset/system inventory with component IDs and criticality
artifact list linking component ID -> architecture diagram/data flow map/documentation (with file identifiers)
document version metadata: last modified timestamp, author, change description
change management records (change request ID, approval timestamp, affected components)
documentation update records linked to change request (update timestamp, updater, diff/commit hash)
scheduled review calendar and completed review sign-offs (review timestamps)
audit/findings records noting undocumented or inconsistent components
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, version-controlled documentation entries linked to change management tickets with timestamps and sign-offs (shows timely, auditable updates).
Strong: full asset-to-documentation linkage and up-to-date architecture/data-flow diagrams in a VCS or documentation system.
Weak: ad-hoc PDF sketches or undated diagrams and manual statements of compliance without change links.
Weak: annual-only review reports without evidence of updates after individual changes.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires documenting and maintaining system descriptions and scheduling updates (proactive measures to prevent unauthorized access/loss), as described by 'compile a comprehensive overview' and 'regular updates'.

CPL-1.differentiation_note: Unlike technical controls that measure detection or remediation, this control requires measuring documentation coverage and timeliness—linking change management events to document updates and tracking percent coverage and time-to-update.

CPL-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and effectiveness of the whistleblower program by tracking percent of employees trained on the policy, count and trend of reports received by channel, median/mean resolution time per case, percent of cases investigated and closed within policy thresholds, and employee awareness survey scores.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is primarily about establishing policy coverage and employee awareness (best evidenced by percentages: percent trained, percent cases closed within policy). Counts and rates (reports received, closure rates) and trends are useful secondary measures to show volume and change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of employees who have completed whistleblower-policy training and acknowledged the policy. 2) For each reported case: channel, submission timestamp, investigation start and close timestamps, outcome (substantiated/unsubstantiated/remediated), and remediation actions. 3) Employee awareness survey scores and response rate. 4) Audit logs from reporting channels (hotline, online portal, third-party vendor) showing receipt and handling of reports.
data_signals: report_submission_timestamp (per case)
report_channel_type (hotline | online_platform | third_party_vendor)
case_investigation_start_timestamp and case_close_timestamp
case_outcome (substantiated | unsubstantiated | dismissed) and remediation_action_logged
case_resolution_time_days
employee_training_completion_records (employee_id, completion_date, module_id)
employee_awareness_survey_scores and survey_response_rate
non-retaliation_complaints_count and follow-up actions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous, system-generated reporting-channel logs with timestamps, case lifecycle records (start/close) and linked remediation artifacts; automated training completion records and periodic awareness survey results with sufficient response rate.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets, single annual manual reports, or anecdotal testimony without timestamps or case files; low survey response rates and missing channel logs weaken evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes a formal policy and channels to prevent retaliation and encourage reporting (phrases: 'establishes a formal framework', 'encourages employees to report', 'training employees'), which is primarily preventive though it includes detective/response elements.

CPL-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on human/policy measures (reporting channels, awareness, case handling) rather than technical controls like access or logging; its measurements are people- and process-centric (training completion, survey scores, case lifecycle metrics) rather than system-configuration or vulnerability metrics.

CRY-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of production database accounts that are unique (non-shared) and have required authentication controls (MFA enabled and compliant password settings), plus counts of shared/service accounts and number of overdue access-review actions to demonstrate enforcement of unique production database authentication.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires coverage measurements (percentage of accounts compliant with uniqueness and MFA). Counts (shared accounts, noncompliant accounts) and rates (review completion rate, frequency of provisioning events) are useful secondary metrics to show scope and timeliness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) total number of production database accounts, (2) number of accounts classified as individual (non-shared) vs shared/service, (3) MFA enrollment/status per account, (4) password-policy compliance per account (last password change, complexity flags), and (5) timestamps of provisioning/deprovisioning and access-review completion. Present percent of accounts meeting 'unique + MFA + password policy' and counts of exceptions and overdue reviews.
data_signals: production database user account inventory with account_id, account_type (individual/service/shared), and assigned owner
authentication configuration exports showing per-account MFA_enabled flag
password policy settings and per-account last_password_change timestamp
provisioning/de-provisioning audit logs (timestamp, actor, action, target_account)
database access logs with user_id, timestamp, source IP (to validate accountability)
access review/audit reports showing review_date, reviewer, actions (disable/modify)
list of privileged/shared accounts and justification/approval records
automation sync logs between IAM and database (provisioning success/failure counts)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated IAM-to-database provisioning records + DB auth configuration showing per-user accounts and MFA flags, plus continuous access logs correlating actions to individual identities.
Strong: regular automated access-review reports showing remediation actions and timestamps rather than ad-hoc spreadsheets.
Weak: one-time screenshots, manual spreadsheets without timestamps, or absence of automated MFA/enforcement logs.

affinity_keywords: review and update user access rights

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control enforces unique accounts and MFA to prevent unauthorized access — described actions (enforce, ensure each user, least privilege) are preventive in nature.

CRY-1.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about ensuring uniqueness and MFA at the production database authentication layer (per-account enforcement and provisioning/deprovisioning evidence), rather than broader IAM policy, encryption, or network access controls; measurements must therefore focus on DB account inventories, DB auth configs, and DB access logs.

CRY-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of encryption keys that are protected by approved key management (HSM/KMS) with RBAC and MFA enforced, the count of unauthorized or anomalous key access attempts, and the median time to revoke or adjust key access after a role change or termination.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage and configuration (best measured as percentages of keys compliant with RBAC/MFA/HSM requirements). Count and rate metrics are useful secondary measures for access attempts and revocation velocity; trending shows improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active encryption keys stored in approved KMS/HSM and assigned RBAC roles with MFA required; count and rate of key access events (authorized vs. unauthorized) with user identity and source; time delta between employee role change/termination event and key access revocation. Also measure frequency of key rotations and the completion rate of scheduled key lifecycle operations.
data_signals: inventory of encryption keys with storage location (HSM/KMS vs. other)
key metadata: creation timestamp, rotation timestamps, destruction timestamp, lifecycle state
key access control lists / role assignments (user/role -> key mapping)
key access event logs including user ID, action (use/export), timestamp, source IP, and MFA assertion
MFA authentication logs correlated to key access events
access revocation events and timestamps correlated to HR role-change/termination records
audit log entries for key access reviews and audit findings with reviewer and timestamp
counts of unauthorized or failed key access attempts and associated alert records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated KMS/HSM audit logs with immutable timestamps and user identifiers, RBAC mappings stored in authoritative IAM, and automated revocation events correlated to HR change feeds
Strong: MFA assertions tied to each key access event in logs
Weak: manual spreadsheets of who 'should' have access, intermittent screenshots of permissions, or annual-only review reports
Weak: access logs missing user identity, MFA evidence, or timestamps that prevent correlation to HR events

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control designed to limit access' and prescribes RBAC, HSM/KMS, MFA, and lifecycle controls to prevent unauthorized key use.

CRY-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets access and lifecycle enforcement for encryption keys (RBAC, MFA, HSM/KMS, revocation SLAs) rather than broader cryptographic practices like algorithm selection or application-level encryption; measurement must focus on key-level ACLs, access events, and revocation timing.

CRY-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of inventoried portable/removable media (USB drives, external hard drives) that have organization-approved encryption (e.g., AES-256) enabled and the count and rate of incidents where unencrypted portable media were involved; additionally measure DLP enforcement events and exceptions to demonstrate policy enforcement and coverage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a coverage/control-compliance requirement (ensure all portable media are encrypted), so percentage (devices encrypted / devices inventoried) is most useful; counts of incidents and trends over time help demonstrate enforcement and remediation velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A continuous inventory of all removable media mapped to users/assets with an encryption_status flag and encryption_algorithm value; DLP enforcement logs showing automatic encryption or block events; incident records linking data loss events to media and noting encryption state; the list of approved exceptions and training completion timestamps for users handling portable media.
data_signals: portable_media_device_inventory records with device_id, assigned_user, device_type (USB/external HDD), OS
encryption_status flag per device (encrypted / unencrypted) and encryption_algorithm field (e.g., AES-256)
auto-encryption configuration flag (automatic encrypt on-write) from endpoint configuration management
Strac Endpoint DLP enforcement logs showing encryption enforcement events, block/allow actions, and timestamps
incident/forensic records that reference portable media involvement and note encryption state
exceptions/approved-device registry with approver, justification, and expiration timestamp
policy assignment records and employee training completion records for removable media handling
audit results showing periodic verification of encryption on sampled or full device sets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated device inventory with real-time encryption_status flags and DLP enforcement logs showing automatic encryption or blocked writes for unencrypted media
Moderate: scheduled full scans/audits that verify encryption on devices combined with DLP summary reports
Weak: point-in-time manual attestations, screenshots, or annual policy statements without machine-readable logs linking device IDs to encryption status

affinity_keywords: policies for the use of removable media
incidents of data loss involving unencrypted media

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires ensuring all portable media are configured to automatically encrypt data (prevents exposure), e.g., 'ensure that all portable media devices ... are configured to automatically encrypt data.'

CRY-3.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on encryption coverage and enforcement for portable/removable media (USB, external HDD) and DLP integration, unlike broader disk or file encryption controls — measurement must tie encryption state to device inventory and DLP enforcement events rather than general endpoint encryption metrics.

CRY-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of sensitive data stores and transmissions that use approved strong encryption (e.g., AES-256 at rest, TLS for in transit) and the key management hygiene (key rotation, access events) — plus counts of exceptions or unencrypted assets against policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage/consistency (best expressed as percentages of assets/flows encrypted). Counts and rates provide complementary visibility (number of unencrypted assets, key rotation rate) and scores can aggregate posture against policy.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percent of assets/datastores tagged sensitive that are encrypted at rest with approved algorithms (e.g., AES-256); (2) percent of external/internal network flows and endpoints using TLS (version/cipher policy compliance); (3) count and percentage of cryptographic keys that meet lifecycle requirements (created, rotated, retired) and the number of unauthorized or unusual key-access/decryption events. Include policy version and exceptions register.
data_signals: asset inventory with sensitive-data tag and encryption_at_rest flag (algorithm metadata, e.g., AES-256)
storage volume / blob metadata showing encryption algorithm and key identifier
TLS/cipher suite scan results per service/endpoint (TLS version, ciphers)
KMS logs: key creation, rotation, deletion timestamps and key usage/access events with principal identity
audit logs showing decryption/key retrieval events with user identity and timestamp
vulnerability assessment or crypto configuration scan results citing weak/deprecated ciphers
encryption policy document version and recorded policy acceptance/training completions
exceptions register listing permitted unencrypted items and compensating controls
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated inventory and KMS logs showing key lifecycle events and authenticated key access tied to principals; automated TLS/cipher scans across endpoints showing policy-compliant ciphers.
Strong: percentage metrics computed from complete sensitive-asset inventory and scan coverage.
Weak: one-time screenshots, attestations without logs, or manual checklist entries; evidence showing weak/deprecated ciphers or missing key rotation despite claimed encryption.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly states 'Data encryption is a preventive control' and focuses on preventing unauthorized access via encryption and key management.

CRY-4.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of cryptographic coverage and key lifecycle (algorithm/version, KMS events) rather than access-review counts or logging/detection metrics — measurement must focus on encryption state and key management evidence rather than general access controls.

CRY-5.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement and coverage of unique user identifiers and MFA: percentage of active user accounts that are unique (non-shared), percentage of accounts with MFA enabled, proportion of accounts disabled within defined timeframes after termination/role change, and frequency/completion rate of scheduled access reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage and compliance (how many accounts meet unique/MFA requirements), so percentage is primary to show coverage rates; counts and rates (e.g., number of shared accounts, deprovisioning rate) are useful secondary measures and trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count active accounts, count accounts flagged as shared/generic, count accounts with MFA enabled, timestamps for account creation and disablement, provisioning/de-provisioning event logs and success/failure codes, results and timestamps of periodic access reviews. Compute: percent of active accounts with unique user identifiers (no generic/shared flag), percent of active accounts with MFA, median time-to-disable after termination, access review completion rate and number of policy violations found per review.
data_signals: centralized directory user records (userID, account_type, creation_timestamp, disable_timestamp)
MFA_enabled flag per account from IAM directory
provisioning and de-provisioning audit logs (request_id, actor, timestamp, outcome)
access review schedules and completion records (review_id, scope, completion_timestamp, exceptions_found)
authentication attempt logs correlated to userID (timestamp, source IP, success/failure)
inventory of shared/service/generic accounts and privileged accounts
onboarding/offboarding HR system event timestamps for termination/role-change
provisioning automation success/failure rates and error messages
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated directory and IAM logs showing MFA flag and unique userIDs with continuous correlation to HR onboarding/offboarding events and automated de-provisioning success records.
Strong: access review records showing scope, issues found, and remediation tracked to closure.
Weak: one-time screenshots or manual spreadsheets of accounts that are not machine-correlated to authentication logs.
Weak: manual provisioning records without timestamps or failure/success codes that prevent measuring timely de-provisioning.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description explicitly calls this a 'preventive control' that enforces unique accounts and MFA to prevent unauthorized access.

CRY-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on enforcing unique user identifiers and MFA coverage plus automated provisioning/de-provisioning evidence, rather than general access policy or role management — measurement must track account uniqueness and deprovisioning timeliness rather than only permission assignments.

DCH-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of customer data items or data stores tied to departed employees that are securely erased within the policy-required timeframe, the count of data retention violations (incidents) related to employee departures, and coverage of deletion actions across inventoried storage locations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires proof of coverage and timeliness (percentage of data deleted within required timeframe) so a percentage metric is primary; counts of deletion actions, incident rates, and trend analysis are useful secondary measures to show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of employee terminations/offboardings and, for each, the set of customer data objects or storage locations mapped to that employee; for each mapped object/location, record deletion action timestamp, erasure method/tool (including NIST 800-88 compliance indicator), and verification result. Compute percent deleted within the policy-required timeframe and list outstanding/not-deleted items with reasons and storage locations.
data_signals: employee termination/offboarding records with timestamp
mapping of employee identifiers to customer data objects or storage locations
data deletion execution logs (tool, command, timestamp, operator)
erasure method metadata indicating NIST 800-88 or certified tool used
deletion verification receipts or cryptographic wipe proofs
access permission change logs (revocations) with timestamps
audit reports or deletion process audit logs
incident records for data retention violations tied to offboarding
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated deletion execution logs and cryptographic/receipt-based verification showing tool and timestamp for each data object, correlated to offboarding record.
Strong: complete data-storage inventory mapping employees to data locations and automated proof-of-erasure per item.
Weak: manual attestations or one-off spreadsheet entries without deletion logs or verification proofs.
Weak: ad-hoc deletion notes without linkage to employee offboarding records or lack of method/standards (no NIST 800-88 evidence).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires establishing a formal deletion policy and procedures to ensure data is erased upon employee departure, which is preventive in nature (preventing unauthorized retention/access).

DCH-1.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets secure erasure tied to employee offboarding and requires mapping deletions to termination events and verification proof—distinct from broader retention or backup policies that focus on retention windows rather than per-employee deletion coverage.

DCH-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of data assets and repositories that have an assigned classification (public/internal/confidential/restricted), track the count and rate of data classification errors found in audits, and report policy review frequency and employee training completion rates to demonstrate the policy is applied and understood.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control that requires coverage/compliance measurements (e.g., percent of assets classified, training completion %). Counts of classification errors and trending over time are useful secondary metrics to show remediation and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percent of items in the authoritative data inventory or repositories with an assigned classification label (public/internal/confidential/restricted); count of classification errors identified by audits; timestamps of last policy review; employee training enrollment and completion records tied to classification training. Measurements should be grouped by data domain/repository and criticality.
data_signals: authoritative data inventory records with classification label fields
metadata/classification tags on data repositories and files (public/internal/confidential/restricted)
audit findings listing data classification errors and remediation timestamps
policy document revision history and policy review timestamps
training attendance and completion records for data classification training
counts of unclassified or unknown-classification assets by repository
mapping of data handling/storage/transmission/disposal controls to classified assets
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated metadata/classification tags in the central data inventory or CMDB are strong evidence; manual spreadsheets without timestamps are weak.
Regular audit reports with drilldowns to asset identifiers and remediation timestamps are strong; ad-hoc or single-point-in-time observations are weak.
Training completion records tied to user IDs and timestamps are strong evidence; anecdotal training attestation without records is weak.
Policy revision history showing scheduled periodic reviews is strong; no documented review cadence or missing timestamps is weak.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control defines a policy and controls to ensure data is categorized and protected (establishes approach/guidelines and training), which prevents misclassification and inappropriate handling.

DCH-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on establishing policy, classification coverage, training, and policy review metrics (coverage and error counts), rather than downstream enforcement metrics like access revocation or encryption efficacy.

DCH-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of non-production environments that contain only anonymized/synthetic data (no production data), count of production-data exports/copies into non-production per reporting period, and the number and timeliness of policy violations or remediation actions when production data appears in non-production.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control about coverage/compliance (so percentage of compliant non-prod environments is most useful). Counts of export/copy events and rates of violations provide complementary detective/corrective evidence and trending shows improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) number of non-production environments flagged in inventory and count of those that are populated with production data vs anonymized/synthetic data; (2) number of production-data export/copy events that target non-production destinations by time window; (3) number of policy violation incidents and time-to-remediate each. Include environment identifiers, dataset IDs, timestamps, user/service account performing the action, and justification/exception ticket if any.
data_signals: environment inventory with environment_type label (production / non-production) and dataset population flag
data classification labels for datasets (sensitive / production / synthetic / anonymized)
export/copy logs (e.g., S3 object copy, DB dump/export events) with source dataset ID, destination environment ID, timestamp, initiating principal
data masking/de-identification tool run logs with dataset ID and masking status/version
access control/IAM grants to production datasets showing principals with non-production access
audit findings and exception ticket records documenting approved deviations
automated monitoring/alert logs that flag attempts to load production data into non-production
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated export/copy logs and monitoring alerts that show zero production-data copy events into non-prod are strong evidence.
Presence of masked/synthetic dataset markers and masking tool run histories for each non-prod environment is strong evidence.
Documented, approved exception tickets with narrow scope and expiration are moderate evidence.
Periodic manual attestations or one-off screenshots without corresponding logs or inventory mappings are weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description focuses on prohibiting use of production data in non-production, creating separate environments, and enforcing access controls—all preventive measures.

DCH-11.differentiation_note: This control uniquely requires measurement of dataset movement/copy events and environment-level population status (masked vs production), unlike sibling controls that may focus only on access reviews or incident detection.

END-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of managed endpoints with an active, up-to-date anti-malware agent reporting telemetry, plus counts and rates of detected malware events and the average time-to-remediate infected endpoints against defined policy targets.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is preventive (ensuring coverage on all devices), so a coverage percentage (endpoints with active, up-to-date agents) is most useful; counts and rates of detections and remediation trends complement coverage to show operational effectiveness as described (detections, remediation time, update frequency).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) total managed endpoints and number reporting an active anti-malware agent and last-definition-update timestamp to derive coverage % and staleness; (2) detection event counts by endpoint, threat name and severity, with timestamps; (3) remediation action records (isolation, removal) and remediation completion timestamps to calculate time-to-remediate; (4) scheduled scan executions and success/failure results; (5) SIEM ingestion logs showing anti-malware alerts forwarded for correlation.
data_signals: endpoint anti-malware agent presence and version per device (agent heartbeat/registration records)
last malware definition/signature update timestamp per endpoint
real-time scan / on-access scan event logs (timestamps, endpoint ID, scan result)
malware detection event logs (timestamp, endpoint ID, threat name, severity, detection method)
remediation action records and timestamps (isolation, cleanup, patching, remediation status)
SIEM ingestion/audit logs for anti-malware alerts (alert id, timestamp, forwarding status)
scheduled vulnerability/anti-malware scan execution logs and success/failure codes
employee phishing/malware awareness training completion records (user ID, completion date)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous agent telemetry and SIEM-ingested detection logs with immutable timestamps (strong)
System-generated remediation action records showing isolation and remediation timestamps (strong)
Regular, machine-readable definition update timestamps across 100% of endpoints (strong)
Intermittent manual reports, screenshots or annual penetration test reports without agent telemetry (weak)
Self-attestation or one-off scans without historical detection/remediation logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description frames anti-malware as a "preventive control" deployed on all devices with automatic updates and real-time scanning to prevent compromise.

END-1.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of endpoint agent deployment, definition-update timeliness, detection event streams, and remediation timelines — unlike sibling controls that might focus on network or application protection, it demands endpoint telemetry and SIEM integration as primary evidence.

GOV-1.measurement_goal: Measure adherence to a scheduled board briefing cadence (e.g., quarterly), the count and percent completion of actionable items arising from briefs within defined SLAs, and board-level training and understanding (survey scores and training completion %) to demonstrate effective governance oversight of cybersecurity and compliance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control emphasizing coverage and adherence (e.g., percent of scheduled briefings held, percent of follow-up actions completed, training completion %). Counts and survey scores are useful secondary measures and trends show ongoing improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of scheduled board briefings held on-schedule per reporting period (e.g., quarter). 2) Count of action items created from briefings and percentage completed within defined SLA (owner, created date, due date, completion date). 3) Board member cybersecurity training completion rate and mean survey/understanding score after briefings. 4) Presence and timestamped distribution of meeting minutes and briefing materials containing stated security metrics (incident response times, compliance audit results, risk assessments).
data_signals: board briefing schedule / calendar entries with planned date and actual meeting timestamps
meeting minutes documents with creation timestamp, attendees list, and distribution receipts
briefing slide decks or dashboard snapshots that include 'security metrics', 'incident response times', 'compliance audit results', 'risk assessments'
action item register tied to briefings: item id, owner, created date, due date, status, completion timestamp
board training records: enrollment timestamps, completion timestamps, course IDs
board member survey responses and aggregated understanding/perceived effectiveness scores
attendance records showing board member presence per briefing
email distribution logs or acknowledgment receipts for briefing materials
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated calendar records + published minutes + action-item tracker with timestamps and status updates showing completion within SLA (demonstrates recurring, auditable process).
Strong: consistent training completion records and repeated improvements in board survey scores across periods (shows effective knowledge transfer).
Weak: ad-hoc emails or single-point-in-time slide snapshots without documented minutes, no action-item owner tracking, or only verbal confirmation of briefings (insufficient for audit).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description calls it a 'preventive control' to ensure leadership engagement and establish schedules, metrics, and training — all prevention-oriented governance activities.

GOV-1.differentiation_note: Unlike operational technical controls, this control requires measuring governance artifacts and human-focused signals (board minutes, action-item closure, training/survey scores) rather than technical telemetry; its unique need is auditing schedule adherence and documented decision/action follow-through.

GOV-2.measurement_goal: Demonstrate the board charter exists in the official governance repository, has an owner, shows evidence of board approval and periodic review, and has been published/communicated to relevant personnel (measured as percent of required governance items current and approved, plus counts of approval/review events).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive governance control, auditors need coverage/recency measures (percentage of required governance artifacts that are current/approved). Counts of approval/review events and trending of review recency serve as secondary confirmation of ongoing governance activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Presence of the board charter file in the authoritative governance repository with metadata fields: document owner, creation date, latest review date, approving board member(s) or meeting minutes reference, version number, publication URL/location, and distribution/communication records (email/intranet posting). Also measure employee acknowledgement/attestation counts linked to the charter and mapping of defined responsibilities to roles.
data_signals: board charter document record (repository path/ID) with version and metadata
document latest review timestamp and prior review history
board approval evidence: signed approval, meeting minutes reference or approval timestamp
publication location URL and publication timestamp (intranet/posting)
distribution/communication logs (email distribution list, notification timestamps)
employee attestation/completion records referencing the charter
assigned owner/responsible role mapping for charter and responsibilities
exceptions or audit findings related to governance documentation
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: board meeting minutes with approval entries + repository metadata showing version, approval timestamp, and published URL, plus communication logs and employee attestations.
Weak: undated/unsigned PDF copies or ad-hoc drafts stored locally without approval metadata; single manual attestations without timestamped distribution records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Documenting the board charter is preventive governance that defines responsibilities and approval processes (establishes policy and roles rather than detecting or correcting events).

GOV-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on board-level charter existence, approval, and communication (evidence: board minutes, approval timestamps, publication and distribution), unlike sibling controls that might target operational policies or technical controls — measurement must emphasize approval metadata and communication/attestation records rather than technical configuration or incident metrics.

GOV-3.measurement_goal: Measure board-level security expertise and governance activity: percent of board members with documented security training/certifications or equivalent evidence, count and frequency of information-security-specific board briefings/agenda items per year, and presence/timeliness of board approvals or reviews of Information Security Policies and role responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control: auditors need coverage metrics (e.g., % of board trained) as the primary signal that expertise exists, with counts (number of briefings, approvals) and trends over time to show ongoing governance activity and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of current board members who completed an organization-approved information security briefing or training in the last 12 months (training record timestamps per person). 2) Count of board meetings per year with documented agenda items or minutes showing review/approval/publication/communication of Information Security Policies. 3) Existence and timestamps of board-level approvals for policy documents and documented assignment of security responsibilities (RACI, job descriptions).
data_signals: board member training completion records with attendee identifier and completion timestamp
board member certifications / resumes showing security experience
board meeting agendas and minutes referencing 'Information Security Policies' with dates and approver names
policy approval records (document version, approver, approval timestamp)
policy publication/distribution logs and acknowledgement receipts from executives
RACI/role definition documents or job descriptions that assign information security responsibilities
count of board-level security briefings or presentations per calendar year
records of security being included in project plan approvals (security sign-off field or checklist timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal training records, signed meeting minutes showing explicit policy review/approval, published policy versions with board approver and distribution receipts.
Weak: informal email notes, undated resumes, or verbal statements without meeting minutes; occasional one-off training without recency or completion timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Developing board expertise prevents failures described in the associated risks (e.g., 'Failure to define, review, approve, publish, share, or communicate Information Security Policies'), so it functions primarily as a preventive governance control.

GOV-3.differentiation_note: Unlike operational controls that measure scan coverage or incident response times, GOV-3 requires board-level governance evidence (training records, meeting minutes, policy approvals) — measures must tie to board member artifacts and governance cadence rather than system telemetry.

GOV-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of scheduled board meetings actually conducted on schedule, the percentage of those meetings that include information security policy review/approval agenda items, and the rate at which policy approvals are published and communicated to personnel within defined policy timeframes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive governance control where auditors expect coverage/fulfillment rates (e.g., % of scheduled meetings held, % of meetings with policy review) as the primary evidence; counts (meeting occurrences, approvals) and trend over time are useful secondary views.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compare scheduled board meeting calendar entries to actual conducted meetings (timestamps) to compute % held on schedule; count meetings that include explicit agenda items for 'Information Security Policies' and capture minutes entries that record policy approvals (policy identifier, version, approver, approval timestamp); measure time from approval timestamp to policy publication and distribution to personnel.
data_signals: board meeting schedule/calendar entries with planned datetime and organizer
actual meeting occurrence records (calendar accepted/held status, meeting start/end timestamps)
meeting agendas including explicit 'Information Security Policies' or 'policy review' line items
meeting minutes documenting approvals with policy identifier, version, approver, and approval timestamp
policy publication logs (published version, publication timestamp)
policy distribution/communication records (email/newsboard distribution lists, acknowledgment receipts)
attendance/quorum records for each meeting
action-item tracker entries tied to meetings (policy review or remediation tasks with due/completion dates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative calendar records + signed minutes containing explicit approval entries + published policy records and distribution receipts
Moderate: meeting agendas and action-item tracker entries without full attendance logs or signed minutes
Weak: undocumented oral confirmation or undated screenshots of meetings with no recorded approvals or publication timestamps

control_type_classification.reasoning: Board meetings are governance activities intended to prevent policy gaps by reviewing and approving information security policies (preventive function).
