GOV-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on governance artifacts (schedules, agendas, minutes, policy publication) rather than technical controls — measurement must capture meeting occurrence, agenda content, approvals, and subsequent policy communications rather than system logs or vulnerability data.

GOV-5.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that a formal data backup policy exists, is approved/published, maps customer data to required backup and recovery requirements (RPO/RTO/retention), and that backups and recovery tests occur and meet the policy-defined requirements (coverage and successful restores).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Input classifies the control as corrective, so primary metrics should measure rates (e.g., policy acknowledgement rate, backup success rate, restore success rate). Percentage and count metrics complement this by showing coverage (policy coverage %) and raw counts of backups/tests.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Existence and currency of the backup policy artifact (version, approver, publish date), percentage of customer-data asset classes mapped to policy backup frequency/RPO/RTO, rate of successful scheduled backups per policy window, and rate/success of periodic recovery tests compared to the policy's recovery requirements.
data_signals: official backup policy document metadata (file, version, last_review_date, approver)
policy distribution/acknowledgement records (employee/service owner acknowledgements, publish date)
customer data classification to backup schedule mapping (asset id → backup frequency, RPO, RTO)
backup job execution logs (job_id, asset_id, start_time, end_time, status, bytes_written)
backup success/failure rate by policy window (per-day or per-scheduled-run)
restore/test exercise results (test_id, asset_scope, success/failure, restore_time, test_date)
inventory of backed-up customer datasets and retention metadata
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: published policy with approver signature and distribution records + continuous backup job logs showing > policy-required success rate and documented restore test successes.
Moderate: recent policy document exists but only limited or ad-hoc backup logs without full coverage mapping to customer data classes.
Weak: policy text only (no approver/date) or one-off manual backup/restore reports without recurring automated evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Input labels the control 'corrective' and the description emphasizes documenting backup and recovery requirements used to restore customer data after incidents ('backup and recovery of customer data').

GOV-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the presence, approval, and documented requirements of a backup/recovery policy (policy artifact, mapping of customer data to RPO/RTO, and test evidence) rather than purely measuring operational backup job counts or broader incident response activities.

GOV-6.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and timeliness of customer notifications for critical system changes: percentage of critical changes for which customers were notified, count of unnotified critical changes, and average time from change approval to customer notification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage (what share of critical changes are notified) and timeliness; percentage shows coverage while counts and trends show volume and change over time against policy expectations.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count critical change records and determine whether each has one or more customer notification events; compute the percentage of critical changes with notifications, the mean time (hours) from change approval to notification, and the count of overdue or missing notifications compared to the notification policy.
data_signals: change ticket records with classification/severity (e.g., 'critical') and approval timestamps
notification records (email logs, portal notification events) with message id, recipient list, and timestamp
list of affected customers per change (customer impact mapping)
notification template/version used and notification channel (email/portal/SMS)
customer acknowledgement/receipt logs (read receipts, ticket replies)
notification policy/SLA or process document defining required timeframe and who to notify
audit trail linking change ticket ID to notification message IDs
post-change incident or operational impact reports referencing notification status
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between change ticket and system-generated notification logs with timestamps and recipient lists; presence of customer acknowledgements; policy-defined SLA timestamps matched to notification timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc/manual emails without change-ticket IDs or missing timestamps; one-off screenshots or retrospective statements without linked logs.
Strong: full mapping of affected customers per critical change showing targeted notifications; Weak: generic announcement copies without per-change recipient lists.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description requires proactively notifying customers of critical changes (prevention of downstream impact), indicating a preventive control.

GOV-6.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on external customer-facing notification coverage and timeliness for critical changes, unlike sibling controls that may address internal change approval or policy documentation; measurement must link change tickets to outbound notifications and customer acknowledgements.

GOV-7.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of organizational roles and positions that have formally documented, approved, and published information security responsibilities, plus the percentage of personnel who have been assigned/acknowledged those responsibilities and the presence of role-based segregation-of-duties controls integrated into project plans.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a coverage/compliance control — auditors typically want percentage coverage (e.g., percent of roles with documented responsibilities and percent of staff who acknowledged), with raw counts and trends to show remediation and improvements over time and comparisons against policy requirements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active job roles with a published, approved responsibilities document (role_description_id mapped to role_id), percentage of employees who have signed/acknowledged their information security responsibilities, count and percentage of project plans that include assigned security responsibilities or risk tasks, and presence/coverage of a segregation-of-duties matrix mapped to active roles.
data_signals: published policy and role-responsibility documents with approval timestamps and version history
HR role catalog / organizational chart with role_id and incumbent mappings
employee acknowledgement/attestation records (signed receipts with timestamps) linked to role_id
project plan artifacts and risk registers showing assigned security responsibilities or sign-offs
segregation-of-duties (SoD) matrix and SoD attestation logs
policy review and approval records (review dates, approver IDs)
remote-work security policy acceptance or configuration attestation records for remote employees
counts of roles without documented responsibilities (open items) and remediation tickets
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative sources (HR system role catalog + versioned approved responsibility documents + employee acknowledgement receipts) and automated linkage between role_id, policy_id, and project tasks.
Strong: SoD matrix with periodic attestations and corrective ticketing for conflicts.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets, unsigned/manual lists, or one-time undocumented verbal assignments.
Weak: absence of approval timestamps or lack of linkage between roles and project plans/SoD controls.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Defining and communicating roles and responsibilities is a preventive governance activity intended to avoid unclear responsibilities (description/name and associated risk text emphasize definition/communication).

GOV-7.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on the existence, approval/publishing, and communication/attestation of management roles and security responsibilities (including SoD and project-plan integration), distinguishing it from sibling controls that may focus on execution, monitoring, or technical access configuration; note: description was empty, so taxonomy is grounded in associated risk statements.

GOV-8.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that an up-to-date organizational structure (org chart) and role/responsibility mappings are documented, formally approved, published/distributed, and periodically reviewed — measured as the percentage of organizational units and active personnel with a current, approved org-chart entry and signed acknowledgement, plus cadence of reviews against the policy-defined schedule.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a coverage/consistency control (documentation and acknowledgement), so percentage metrics (coverage/acknowledgement rates) best show compliance; counts of documents/approvals and trend over time (review cadence) provide additional context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of organizational units and active employees that have a current org-chart entry and assigned role/responsibility mapping with an approval timestamp and publication record; count of org-chart/document versions and their approval timestamps; percentage of employees who have electronically acknowledged receipt or review; timestamp of last formal review compared to the policy review schedule.
data_signals: org-chart/document version metadata (document_id, version, created_at, updated_at)
document approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp, approval_signature)
publication/publish_timestamp and publication location (intranet page, repository URL)
employee acknowledgement receipts/logs (employee_id, acknowledgement_timestamp, ack_method)
role-to-responsibility mapping entries (role_id, responsibilities, assigned_owner)
document review schedule entries and last_review_date per document or organizational unit
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed/recorded approval entries and automated acknowledgement logs with timestamps and employee IDs, versioning history, and scheduled review records demonstrating periodic updates.
Weak: single archived document file without approval metadata, no publication or acknowledgement records, or manual screenshots of the org chart lacking timestamps and version history.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Documenting and publishing organizational structure is preventive because it establishes roles/responsibilities to prevent misunderstandings; supported by associated risk text about failure to define/review/communicate policies.

GOV-8.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documentation, approval, publication, and acknowledgement of org structure and role mappings (people/process evidence) rather than technical enforcement or logging — measurement must capture approvals, publication, and employee acknowledgement rather than system telemetry.

GOV-10.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of documented roles and assigned information security responsibilities: percentage of active organizational roles with a documented security responsibility statement, count of job descriptions and project plans that include named security owners, and percentage of role-responsibility records reviewed and approved within the required review period.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage verification (best expressed as percentages of roles covered by documented responsibilities). Counts (number of roles, job descriptions, projects) and trend metrics (reviews over time) are useful secondary evidence to show ongoing maintenance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of active roles listed in the central role/responsibility registry that have an associated documented information security responsibility clause and an approval timestamp; count of job descriptions and project plan artifacts that reference a named security owner; percentage of role records with last_review_date within policy-defined review window.
data_signals: role responsibility registry entries: (role_id, responsibility_text, assigned_owner, created_date, last_review_date, approval_signature_id)
job description documents indexed with security_responsibility_flag and last_revision_date
project plan artifacts / RACI matrices showing assigned security owners for security tasks
document approval/publish records (approver_id, approval_date, published_version)
training/acknowledgement logs linking personnel to role_id and acknowledgement_timestamp
audit or change log of role/responsibility updates (change_id, changed_by, change_timestamp, change_reason)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized, queryable role-responsibility registry with approval timestamps and last_review_date entries; project plans and RACI matrices consistently referencing registry role_ids; signed approvals and published policy versions.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads, undocumented manager knowledge, or isolated PDFs lacking approval metadata or review timestamps.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires specifying/allocating roles and responsibilities (a preventive measure to avoid unclear responsibilities), as indicated by 'Roles and responsibilities specified' and associated risks about responsibilities not being defined or allocated.

GOV-10.differentiation_note: This control focuses on documenting and approving role-level security responsibilities across the organization (coverage and review cadence), unlike sibling controls that may address technical enforcement, logging, or incident response; its unique measurement need is coverage and approval metadata (registry entries, sign-offs, review dates).

GOV-11.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required Information Security Policies that are approved, assigned an owner, published, and reviewed within the organization-defined review period, plus counts of overdue reviews and employee acknowledgements; also measure how often policies are referenced in project plans and assigned responsibilities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (policy existence, review, and communication) so percentage metrics (coverage and on-time review %) best show compliance; counts and trends help show volume of overdue policies and improvement over time, and a score can aggregate multiple attributes (approval, publication, acknowledgement).

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of active security policies with a recorded approver and last_review_date within the policy-defined review interval; (2) count of policies published to the employee population and the employee acknowledgement completion rate within X days of publication; (3) count of policies referenced in active project plans and count of policies missing an assigned owner or responsibilities.
data_signals: policy document metadata (policy_id, title, version)
policy last_review_date and next_review_due_date
policy approver_id / approval signature or approval timestamp
policy publication_date and publication channel (intranet/email)
employee policy acknowledgement records (employee_id, ack_timestamp)
policy owner / responsible role assignment
mapping of policy_id referenced in project plans or project artifacts
records of fines / regulatory non-compliance related to policy gaps (if present) / incidents attributed to policy absence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated policy metadata with approver and review timestamps, centralized publication logs, and system-recorded employee acknowledgements (high trust, auditable).
Strong: consistent project artifacts referencing policy IDs and assigned owners showing integration into planning.
Weak: single point-in-time PDF copies stored in shared drive without review dates or approver signatures.
Weak: ad-hoc email announcements without tracked acknowledgements or missing owner assignments.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on establishing, reviewing, approving and communicating policies, which are preventive governance activities (creation and maintenance of policy reduces future risk).

GOV-11.differentiation_note: This control is about the policy lifecycle, publication, and personnel awareness — unlike technical controls it requires measuring document metadata, acknowledgements, and policy-to-project mappings rather than system configuration or detection events.

GOV-12.measurement_goal: Measure the operational availability of the organization's support/incident contact system and the completeness and currency of incident response contact information so auditors can see uptime, successful notification delivery, and the percentage of personnel/roles with up-to-date contacts within required thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Availability and completeness are best expressed as percentages (uptime %, contact list coverage %); secondary counts/rates capture outages, notification success rates and ticket/response rates as additional evidence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Support system availability percentage (uptime) over the audit period and count of availability incidents with start/end timestamps; 2) Percentage of required incident response contacts present and timestamped in the canonical contact directory; 3) Notification delivery success rate and mean time to acknowledge/first-response for breach-related tickets compared to policy thresholds.
data_signals: support system uptime / availability metrics (start/end, duration)
support/incident system incident logs (outage events with root-cause tags)
canonical incident/contact directory entries with last-updated timestamps and role mapping
notification delivery receipts (email/SMS/push) and delivery/failure status for breach notifications
helpdesk/ticketing records for breach-related tickets with first-response and resolution timestamps
policy/publication timestamps showing last publish or review of incident contact procedures
count of contacts missing required fields or marked stale/overdue for review
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous availability monitoring (automated uptime metrics) plus delivery receipts and automated reconciliation showing >95% contact coverage and up-to-date timestamps.
Strong: automated notification logs tied to canonical contact directory and ticketing events for breach response.
Weak: occasional manual email confirmations, spreadsheets without timestamps, or one-off screenshots of contact lists.
Weak: single point-in-time attestations without trending or incident logs for outages and delivery failures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name and associated risks emphasize availability and maintained contacts to prevent delays in breach response, which aligns with preventive measures ensuring readiness and coverage.

GOV-12.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on operational availability and the currency/completeness of support/contact information (uptime and contact-directory evidence) rather than broader policy content or technical vulnerability remediation metrics.

GOV-13.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of implemented system changes for which a communication was sent to the full set of authorized internal users within the required timeframe, plus the count of acknowledged vs. unacknowledged change notifications and the count of missed or overdue communications.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is about coverage — percent of changes communicated to authorized users is the clearest coverage metric; counts of notifications and trending over time supplement coverage and show recurrence or improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each change ticket, record whether a communication was issued (message ID and timestamp), which authorized users were targeted and which acknowledged receipt; compute percent of changes with successful delivery to full authorized roster and count of overdue/missed communications against the organizational policy timeframe.
data_signals: change ticket ID and change implementation timestamp
communication message ID and sent timestamp linked to change ticket
recipient list for each communication mapped to authorized internal user roster
delivery receipts / bounce/failure logs for each communication
acknowledgement receipts (read/accept confirmations) per recipient
change communication policy or documented timeframe requirement
overdue or missed-communication flags tied to change ticket
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated communication logs with message IDs, delivery receipts, and per-user acknowledgement records linked to change ticket records.
Strong: mapping showing recipient lists reconciled to the authoritative authorized-user roster and policy-defined timeframe.
Weak: ad-hoc manual attestations or unsourced screenshots of a single email thread without delivery/acknowledgement metadata.
Weak: periodic one-off attestations that cannot be reconciled to each change ticket or to the authorized-user list.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description 'communicates system changes to authorized internal users' is proactive and aims to prevent misunderstanding or insecure activity by informing personnel, aligning with preventive classification.

GOV-13.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on the communication and acknowledgement of changes (notification coverage and delivery evidence), unlike sibling change controls that might focus on approval, testing, or rollback — measurement must link communications to change tickets and authorized-user rosters.

HRS-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of new hires (including contractors where applicable) with a completed background check recorded prior to or on their employment start date, and the rate of exceptions/waivers by role and information-classification.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors expect coverage metrics (percentage of hires screened). Supplement with counts of completed checks, exception rates, and trends over time to show ongoing compliance and remediation of gaps.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of new-hire records per period, count of those with background_check_completed flag and background_check_completion_timestamp, role and information-classification for each hire, and count of documented exceptions/waivers. Also capture background check scope/type and whether completion occurred prior to or on employee_start_date to verify timing requirements.
data_signals: candidate_id / employee_id
background_check_completion_timestamp
background_check_status (completed / pending / failed / waived)
background_check_scope/type (criminal, employment verification, education, credit) or policy level
employee_start_date / onboarding_date
role / job_title and associated information classification or access level
documented exceptions or waivers with approver and timestamp
contractor_flag and contractor_onboarding_records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HRIS records showing background_check_completion_timestamp before employee_start_date, linked to candidate_id and role, plus signed exception records when applicable.
Weak: ad-hoc emailed confirmations, manual attestations, or annual summary reports without per-hire timestamps or role mapping.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description 'performs background checks on new employees' indicates a control applied to prevent hiring of risky individuals before access is granted.

HRS-1.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on pre-employment screening coverage and timing (checks completed prior to start) rather than downstream access reviews or termination processes, so measurements must map checks to hires, roles, and information-classification for proportionality.

HRS-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of documented Code of Conduct acknowledgements for all active contractors (percentage acknowledged), the count of contractors missing or overdue acknowledgements, and proof that acknowledgements reference confidentiality and termination obligations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control is best evidenced by coverage percentage (what percent of contractors have signed/acknowledged the Code). Counts of missing acknowledgements and trending of acknowledgement rates over time are useful secondary measures to show remediation and onboarding compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active contractors with a recorded Code of Conduct acknowledgement in the HR/contractor management system (acknowledgement record present and timestamped), count of contractors without acknowledgement, timestamps of acknowledgement relative to contract start date, document version acknowledged, and any linkage to confidentiality/termination clauses.
data_signals: contractor roster with unique contractor ID and active/inactive status
Code of Conduct acknowledgement records (acknowledgement_timestamp, ack_method e.g., e-signature, document_version)
contract start_date and onboarding completion timestamp
termination records indicating confidentiality/termination communication delivered
list of contractors with missing or overdue acknowledgements
audit trail of changes to acknowledgement records (who recorded, IP, timestamp)
policy definition or policy_threshold for required acknowledgement (e.g., required within X days of start)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/contract management system records with unique contractor IDs, timestamped e-signature entries, document version stored, and audit trail showing acknowledgement before or on contract start.
Strong: system-generated reports showing 100% coverage or named exceptions with justification.
Weak: ad-hoc scanned PDFs or unsigned emailed statements without timestamped linkage to the contractor record.
Weak: periodic manual attestations without system audit logs or missing document versioning.

affinity_keywords: acquire, use, or dispose of assets

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring contractors to acknowledge a Code of Conduct is a preventive action to reduce insider, confidentiality, and policy-awareness risks (terms like 'acknowledged' and 'Confidentiality commitments' imply prevention).

HRS-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurable, documented acknowledgements from contractors (coverage and timeliness); unlike sibling people-controls (background checks or training) it focuses on signed policy acknowledgement records and their linkage to contractor lifecycle events.

HRS-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of employees who have formally acknowledged the Code of Conduct and the rate and disposition of enforcement actions tied to Code violations (onboarding, periodic re-acknowledgement, and at termination).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (acknowledgement/coverage) so percentage (acknowledgement coverage) is most useful; counts and rates capture enforcement events and trends in violations over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percent of active employees with a recorded Code of Conduct acknowledgment (by employee_id, timestamp, and policy version) and (2) count and rate of enforcement actions (investigations, disciplinary actions, remedial trainings) tied to Code violations, including termination-stage confidentiality acknowledgement. Break down by employee type (employee/contractor), location (remote/on-site), and time period.
data_signals: employee_id with Code of Conduct acknowledgment timestamp and policy_version
onboarding/offboarding checklist entries noting Code acknowledgement at hire/termination
signed/confidentiality agreements recorded at termination
HR training completion records linked to Code of Conduct
disciplinary action records tagged with Code violation reason and resolution date
reminder/escalation logs for overdue acknowledgements
employee status (active/terminated/contractor) and role/classification
counts of reported breaches or incidents referencing Code violations
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: system-captured acknowledgements with unique employee identifiers, timestamps, and policy versioning from the HR/LMS system; automated reminders and escalation logs; cross-linked disciplinary records showing enforcement outcomes.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or unsigned screenshots of signoff, anecdotal attestations, or annual manual attestations without timestamps or linkage to employee records.

affinity_keywords: acquire, use, or dispose of assets

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires acknowledgements and enforcement (prevention of misconduct) — 'acknowledged by employees and enforced' signals a preventive policy/compliance control.

HRS-3.differentiation_note: This control focuses on evidence of policy acknowledgement and operational enforcement (acknowledgement timestamps, enforcement actions) rather than upstream hiring checks or technical access controls referenced in related risks.

HRS-4.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of contractors who have a recorded, dated confidentiality agreement signature on file at or before their engagement start date, the count of contractors lacking a signed agreement, and the count of agreements signed after engagement (exceptions).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control requiring coverage at onboarding, so percentage (coverage of contractors with signed agreements) is the most useful metric; counts of unsigned contractors and trends over time are useful secondary measures to track exceptions and improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of current and newly engaged contractors with a recorded confidentiality agreement signature timestamped on or before their engagement start date. Also measure the raw count of contractors without a signature, the count signed after engagement, and the number of termination records that include communicated confidentiality commitments.
data_signals: contractor identifier (employee/contractor ID)
engagement start date (HR record)
confidentiality agreement signed timestamp
agreement version or template ID
signature method (e-signature log or scanned document reference)
HR onboarding record linking to signed agreement
unsigned contractor list (contractor records missing signed agreement field)
termination/exit checklist entry showing confidentiality commitments communicated
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HR/onboarding system records with e-signature timestamp <= engagement start date and agreement template ID linked to contractor record; termination checklist entries showing confidentiality communicated.
Weak: scanned PDFs with no timestamp or manually maintained spreadsheets, or post-hoc signatures recorded after engagement without justification.
Additional strength: low or zero count of unsigned contractors and documented exception approvals for any late/missing signatures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control 'requires contractors to sign a confidentiality agreement at the time of engagement,' which is an action to prevent unauthorized disclosure, so it's preventive.

HRS-4.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires proof of signature at onboarding (signature timestamp relative to engagement date) rather than broader personnel checks or policy publication — measurements must link agreement signatures to engagement events and track any post-engagement or missing signatures.

HRS-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of new hires who have a signed confidentiality agreement captured in the HR record within the onboarding period and the count of outstanding or late signings; verify stored signed agreement artifacts (timestamped signatures and agreement version) for each employee.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control that requires coverage/compliance across all new employees, so a percentage of hires with signed agreements is the most direct measure; counts of unsigned hires and trends over time are useful secondary signals to detect gaps or improvements.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Ratio of new employees (by hire date) with a recorded signed confidentiality agreement within the defined onboarding timeframe; count of employees hired in the period without a signed agreement; evidence artifacts linking employee ID to a signed agreement file with timestamp and agreement version. Include exception/waiver records where applicable.
data_signals: employee_id
hire_date (HRIS)
confidentiality_agreement_signed_timestamp
signed_agreement_file_reference or stored PDF
agreement_version_id or template identifier
onboarding_checklist completion status
exceptions_or_waiver records for missing signatures
list/count of new hires without signed agreement
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HRIS record linking employee_id to a timestamped signed agreement file and agreement_version_id captured at onboarding.
Strong: consistent onboarding checklist entries and no outstanding unsigned new hires within the policy timeframe.
Weak: occasional scanned PDFs without signature metadata, or manual attestations with no timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc or sampled evidence (e.g., single PDF) rather than coverage across all new hires.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control 'requires employees to sign a confidentiality agreement during onboarding,' which is a preventive action to stop unauthorized disclosure before it occurs.

HRS-5.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on capture and retention of signed confidentiality agreements at onboarding (signature presence, timestamp, stored artifact), unlike sibling people-controls that focus on background checks, policy publication, or termination communications.

HRS-6.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of required performance evaluations (percent of employees with completed evaluations within the required cycle), count and age of overdue evaluations, and evidence that evaluations/termination processes include communicated confidentiality commitments and links to security awareness/training outcomes.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control is about coverage and completion of periodic performance evaluations so percentage (completion rate) is the primary verifier; counts (overdue / completed) and trends over cycles help demonstrate timeliness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of employees with a completed performance evaluation within the required period (e.g., annual cycle), count of overdue evaluations and mean/median days overdue, and the presence of documented confidentiality acknowledgements at termination. Break these measures down by role/privilege tier and by termination vs active employees so an auditor can verify coverage and linkage to security requirements.
data_signals: performance evaluation records (employee_id, evaluator_id, evaluation_date, evaluation_period)
evaluation due date and completion timestamp (scheduled_date, completed_timestamp)
overdue evaluation list and days overdue
signed evaluation forms or HRIS attestations (manager and employee signatures)
termination checklist entries showing confidentiality commitment acknowledgement (employee_id, signed_timestamp)
security awareness training completion records (employee_id, module_id, completion_date)
background check / pre-employment verification status (employee_id, verification_date, scope)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated HRIS records with timestamps and stored signed evaluation/termination acknowledgement documents, plus system-generated overdue reports and reminder history.
Strong: linkage between evaluation outcomes and training completion records (showing action taken for knowledge gaps).
Weak: one-off manual spreadsheets or manager attestations without timestamps or supporting signed documents.
Weak: annual summary reports without per-employee records or evidence of confidentiality communication at termination.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title and associated risks indicate proactive staff management (ensuring evaluations and communicated confidentiality), which prevents insider risk and policy non‑compliance.

HRS-6.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measuring HR evaluation completion and evidence of confidentiality/termination communications tied to security outcomes, rather than solely measuring background check coverage or policy publication; it therefore needs HRIS/timestamped evaluation and termination acknowledgement signals.

IAC-1.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of production application accounts and sessions that are legitimately authorized and the rate of unauthorized access attempts blocked; demonstrate that production application access configurations and provisioning actions enforce 'authorized only' access policy.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percent of accounts compliant) to show enforcement; counts of blocked attempts and trends over time provide complementary detection and remediation context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of production-application accounts that are active and have an approved authorization record, the count and rate of denied/blocked access attempts to production applications, and the proportion of access provisioning/deprovisioning events that follow the approved workflow and have attestation. Include mappings to production application inventory so measurements are scoped to production systems.
data_signals: production application access control list / entitlement roster (account, role, environment tag)
authorization/approval records for each account (requestor, approver, timestamp, justification)
authentication logs filtered for production applications (success/failure, timestamp, user, source IP)
access denial / blocked attempt events for production applications
provisioning and deprovisioning logs for production accounts (create/modify/disable timestamps and change request IDs)
privileged account registry for production applications and role assignments
session establishment logs showing environment tag = production
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated IAM configuration exports + continuous auth/access-denial logs showing zero successful unauthorized sessions for production and traceable approval records for each active account
Strong: provisioning workflows with request/approval IDs linked to account creation timestamps
Weak: one-time screenshots of access lists or annual attestations without timestamped authentication logs
Weak: manual spreadsheets of access without linkage to production environment identifiers or audit logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: Phrases like 'access restricted' and 'authorized access only' indicate this control is intended to prevent unauthorized access before it occurs.

IAC-1.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets production-application access enforcement and provisioning (not general network or endpoint controls), so measurements must be scoped to production application accounts, sessions, and provisioning workflows rather than broad system-wide metrics.

IAC-2.domain: Access control / Identity and access management
IAC-2.sub_domain: access_control_procedures_and_enforcement (including segregation_of_duties and remote access procedures)
IAC-2.measurement_goal: Measure the existence and operational enforcement of documented access-control procedures by tracking: percentage of access provisioning/removal actions that followed documented request-and-approval procedures within SLA, completion rate of scheduled access reviews, and count of segregation-of-duties conflicts detected and remediated within defined timelines.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control: auditors want coverage/compliance percentages (procedures applied, reviews completed). Counts and rates (conflicts, requests processed, remediation actions) supplement percentage measures and trending shows ongoing enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Quantify procedural enforcement by: 1) counting access provisioning/removal events and verifying each has a corresponding approved access request record and approver ID within the SLA window; 2) measuring percent of accounts/entitlements reviewed and attested in the last review cycle; 3) counting segregation-of-duties (SoD) conflict instances and the percent remediated within required timeframes; 4) tracking remote access procedure adherence (e.g., documented remote access approvals and configuration checks).
data_signals: access policy / procedure document identifier and effective date
access request records (access_request_id, requester_id, request_timestamp, requested_entitlements)
approval records (approver_id, approval_timestamp) linked to provisioning events
provisioning/provisioned_event logs (account_id, entitlement_changes, provisioning_timestamp, operator_id)
access review/attestation records (review_cycle_id, reviewer_id, completion_timestamp, exceptions_list)
segregation-of-duties conflict reports (conflict_id, conflicting_entitlements, detection_timestamp)
SoD remediation tickets and resolution timestamps (remediation_action_id, resolved_timestamp)
remote access approval records and remote access session logs referencing approval IDs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage of provisioning events to approved access requests and approver IDs with timestamps and SLA fields; continuous access-review records showing cycle completion and exception remediation history.
Weak: standalone policy documents or one-off manual attestations without system logs tying approvals to provisioning, or infrequent manual spreadsheets of approvals.
Strong: SoD conflict detection from automated role/entitlement analysis and tracked remediation tickets; Weak: anecdotal or undocumented SOD statements.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes access-control procedures intended to prevent unauthorized access and conflicts (phrases: 'Access control procedures established', focus on segregation of duties and prevention of breaches).

IAC-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on existence and enforcement of documented access procedures (approvals, provisioning, reviews, SoD) rather than on detection or incident response — measurements must prove procedural application (linked approvals → provisioning), not just logging or post-incident analysis.

IAC-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of privileged database accounts that are assigned only to authorized users with an approved business-need and the count of privileged accounts without an approved justification or without required controls (MFA, owner).
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage/compliance metrics (percentage of privileged DB accounts with approved business-need and required protections). Counts and rates supplement by showing raw numbers of exceptions and provisioning activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) number of privileged database roles/accounts with a documented, approved business-need divided by total privileged database accounts; (b) count of privileged accounts lacking approval or owner; (c) proportion of privileged accounts enforced with required controls (MFA, managed via IAM). Include timestamps for approvals and provisioning events to show recency.
data_signals: database privileged role/account membership export (role, account_id, assigned_user or service_account)
access approval/request records with business justification, approver, and approval timestamp
provisioning/deprovisioning events for DB privileged accounts (actor, action, timestamp)
database session/connection logs for privileged accounts (user, source IP, start/end, success/failure)
MFA/enrollment or authentication configuration status for privileged accounts
periodic privileged access review reports (reviewer, reviewed_accounts, completion_timestamp)
list of privileged accounts without linked owner or without an approval record (orphaned privileged accounts)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated exports showing current privileged-role membership tied to recorded approval requests with timestamps and enforcement of MFA/policy at authentication time; periodic signed access review reports.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or one-off screenshots of approvals, lack of provisioning event logs, or approvals older than review policy period.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states the company 'restricts privileged access ... to authorized users with a business need,' indicating preventive access controls and provisioning restrictions.

IAC-3.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on privileged database accounts and proof of business-need approvals and enforcement (e.g., MFA, owner), rather than general system access or logging — measurement must tie DB privileged-role membership to approval records and provisioning events.

IAC-4.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of firewall administrative/privileged accounts that have an active, documented authorization (business justification and owner) and are provisioned only when needed; report the count of privileged firewall accounts without valid authorization and the number of unauthorized privileged access events over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/compliance: auditors will want a percentage of privileged firewall accounts with valid authorization (coverage). Counts of unauthorized accounts and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show exposure and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percent = (number of firewall admin/privileged accounts with an active authorization record documenting business need and owner) / (total number of firewall admin/privileged accounts). Also measure the raw count of privileged accounts lacking authorization, and the count of privileged access assignments or elevation events lacking approval within the change/ticketing system.
data_signals: firewall administrative/privileged account inventory (account id, role, creation timestamp)
authorization records / access request tickets documenting business need and approver for each privileged account
provisioning and deprovisioning timestamps for firewall admin accounts
access review logs / attestations for firewall privileged accounts (completion timestamp, reviewer)
authentication logs for privileged accounts (last login timestamp, source IP, session duration)
change management records for privileged access changes (ticket id, approval status)
alerts or logs of unauthorized privileged access attempts or unexpected privileged sessions
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: authoritative firewall admin inventory automatically reconciled with the ticketing/IAM system showing active authorization records and automated provisioning/deprovisioning; continuous authentication logs tied to account IDs.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots, manually maintained spreadsheets of admins, or annual attestations without matching provisioning/authentication logs.
Moderate: periodic access review documents without corresponding provisioning timestamps or authentication evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control states the company 'restricts privileged access' to authorized users with a business need, which is a preventive access control measure.

IAC-4.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about managing and authorizing firewall privileged/admin accounts (authorization and provisioning evidence), not about firewall rule correctness, traffic monitoring, or vulnerability scanning — measurement must focus on privileged-account authorization records and reconciliation with provisioning/authentication logs.

IAC-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of production hosts that enforce OS-level access restrictions (only authorized accounts, restricted remote protocols, and centralized privileged access) and count unauthorized or privileged access events against production systems to demonstrate enforcement and detect violations.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (restricting access) so coverage/percentage metrics (hosts compliant vs total) best show enforcement; counts/rates of unauthorized access attempts and trends over time provide complementary detective evidence of failures or attacks.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of production hosts (tagged as production) that have only approved interactive accounts and disallow direct unmanaged remote access (SSH/RDP) by unauthorized principals; additionally measure counts of failed/successful authentication attempts from non-approved accounts and the number of production hosts with local privileged accounts or open root/administrator access. Metrics should be grouped by host identifier, account ID, access method (SSH/RDP/local), and a production tag to allow queries such as: (hosts_with_only_authorized_os_accounts / total_production_hosts) and count(unauthorized_os_auth_attempts) per day.
data_signals: production host inventory with tags/labels identifying environment
list of local and centralized accounts with interactive/shell access on each production host (usernames, account source e.g., local/AD/LDAP)
sudoers/group membership and local admin/administrator account lists on production hosts
authorized_keys files and SSH public key listings on production hosts
host firewall/security-group rules permitting SSH/RDP to production hosts
bastion/jump host access logs and successful session records
authentication logs from production hosts (successful and failed SSH/RDP/logon events) with source IP and timestamp
privileged account provisioning/change request records and approvals
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, continuous inventory and authentication logs correlated to a production tag (strong) vs. ad-hoc screenshots or single manual access review (weak)
Proof of centralized, enforced access path (bastion + MFA) and absence of direct SSH/RDP openings to production hosts (strong) vs. presence of open security-group rules or unmanaged keys (weak)
Trend of zero unauthorized successful logins and low/declining unauthorized attempt rate (strong) vs. isolated point-in-time checks showing compliance (weaker)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name 'Production OS access restricted' indicates prevention by restricting who/what may access production operating systems.

IAC-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on OS-level access controls for production hosts (accounts, SSH/RDP, privileged user lists and jump-host usage), unlike sibling controls that may address network, application, or data access which require different signals (e.g., firewall rules or application auth logs).

IAC-6.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of production network assets and paths that are restricted to approved access methods and identities, plus counts of direct/unauthorized access attempts to production resources. Specifically: percent of production hosts/subnets behind approved network controls and number of active accounts or sessions with direct network access to production.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (restricting access) so coverage percentages (e.g., percent of production assets behind approved controls) best show compliance; counts and rates (unauthorized attempts, active direct-access accounts) are useful secondary detective measures and trends show improvement or regression over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate: (1) percentage of production subnets/hosts that have only approved ingress rules (firewall/ACL/security groups) and require approved access methods; (2) count of user accounts/roles with network-level access to production; (3) count/rate of network sessions into production from unapproved sources or without required controls (MFA/JIT). Include access request/approval records for privileged production access to validate allowed exceptions.
data_signals: production subnet and host inventory (tagged as production)
firewall rule sets and network ACLs for production subnets (allow/deny entries)
security group / VPC configuration for production resources
VPN / bastion / jump-host session logs with source IP, username, timestamp
access request and approval records for production network access (requestor, approver, timestamps, justification)
authentication/MFA enforcement logs for production access methods
counts of accounts/roles granted network-level access to production (IAM bindings)
endpoint posture or remote-work compliance reports (for insecure remote work site risk)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, continuous network configuration and access logs (firewall/ACL/security-group configs in infrastructure-as-code), bastion/VPN session logs with MFA status, and up-to-date access approval records tied to identities.
Weak: periodic screenshots, ad-hoc emails approving access, or annual attestations without matching runtime network logs or session records.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name uses 'restricted' for production network access, indicating it is designed to prevent unauthorized access to production environments.

IAC-6.differentiation_note: This control is focused specifically on network-level restrictions for production environments and remote-site risk mitigation (network segmentation, firewall/ACL configuration, and approved access paths), rather than identity lifecycle, logging completeness, or vulnerability remediation which siblings might address.

IAC-7.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of entitled user accounts and privileged accounts that have completed scheduled access reviews on time, the count of access removals or entitlement changes resulting from each review, and the number of overdue or exception reviews per review cycle.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Access reviews are a preventive control that verify coverage and compliance; percentage metrics (coverage/completion rate) directly show whether review policy thresholds are met, while counts and rates track removals, overdue items, and review frequency over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each review cycle, report: total number of accounts scoped, number and percentage of accounts with a completed attestation (with reviewer identity and timestamp), number of entitlement removals or deactivations resulting from the review (with provisioning change timestamps), and count of overdue or exception reviews. Include mapping of review scope to systems (privileged vs standard) and reviewer assignments.
data_signals: access review schedule and completion timestamps (attestation logs)
list of accounts scoped per review (account inventory with system mapping)
review outcome records (approve/deny/remove) including reviewer identity
provisioning/deprovisioning change logs with timestamps and change reason
privileged account list and role assignments at time of review
overdue review reports and exception approval records
number of entitlement removals or access changes resulting from review
review cycle identifier and coverage percentage by system
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated attestation logs tied to identity/provisioning systems (IAM, SCIM, LDAP) plus provisioning change records showing removals/deactivations within defined windows.
Weak: point-in-time spreadsheets, one-off screenshots, or manual attestations without linking to provisioning change logs or timestamps.
Strong: reviewer identity and timestamped attestations covering all scoped systems; Weak: missing reviewer identity or undated attestations.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Access reviews conducted' indicates recurring review/recertification activity intended to prevent unauthorized access by removing inappropriate entitlements.

IAC-7.differentiation_note: This control is focused on measuring review completion, coverage and resulting entitlement changes (attestation + provisioning evidence) rather than detection or incident response metrics, so it requires attestation and provisioning-change signals rather than only alert logs.

IAC-8.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of terminated employees/contractors whose logical and physical access were revoked within the organization-defined timeframe (offboarding policy), plus the mean time-to-revoke and number of orphaned accounts by system.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control asserting coverage/timeliness: auditors need percentage coverage (percent of terminations fully deprovisioned on time) as primary evidence, with counts of failed deprovisions, rates of on-time revocation, and trending to show sustained compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute per termination event: (1) whether each terminated identity had all assigned logical and physical accesses revoked, (2) the time delta between HR termination timestamp and final access removal per system, and (3) the count of active accounts after termination (orphaned accounts) grouped by system and privilege level. Report percent of terminations with complete revocation within policy timeframe and mean time-to-revoke.
data_signals: HR termination event records (employee_id, termination_timestamp, termination_reason)
IAM deprovisioning logs (account_id, disable_timestamp, operator/system)
active account inventory with status flags per system (active/disabled) and linked employee_id
time-to-deprovision per terminated user (computed difference: disable_timestamp - termination_timestamp)
last_login timestamps and orphaned account list (accounts linked to terminated employees still active)
physical access badge deactivation logs (badge_id, deactivation_timestamp)
VPN/remote access credential revocation logs (credential_id, revocation_timestamp)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, timestamped IAM and badge deprovisioning logs correlated with HR termination events (strong evidence).
System-by-system automated deprovisioning records showing disable_timestamp per account (strong).
Manual spreadsheets or a one-time attestation without correlated timestamps (weak evidence).
Presence of orphaned accounts or accounts disabled > policy_threshold after termination (weak/failed evidence).

control_type_classification.reasoning: Revoking access upon termination is intended to prevent unauthorized access and compromise, so it functions as a preventive control.

IAC-8.differentiation_note: This control specifically focuses on timeliness and completeness of offboarding/deprovisioning (including physical badge and remote access revocation) rather than initial provisioning or periodic access reviews, so measurements must correlate HR termination events with per-system deprovisioning timestamps.

IAC-9.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of access grants that are backed by an approved access request and the count of access grants performed without a recorded request; additionally measure time-to-approval for access requests to show the approval gating is enforced.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily about coverage/compliance (every access must have a request) so percentage (coverage of access grants tied to approved requests) is most useful; counts of unauthorized grants and trends over time are useful secondary signals to detect incidents and operational impact.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of access provisioning events (by system and access type) that include a linked, approved access request ID; also report the raw count of provisioning events lacking any request, and the distribution of approval lead times (request submission to approval timestamp). For remote/VPN access, include a filter to show requests tied to remote work site access.
data_signals: access request records (request ID, requester identity, requested resource, request timestamp)
approval records (approver identity, approval timestamp, approval decision/justification)
provisioning/change execution logs that include request ID or ticket reference
access grant events (user, resource, grant timestamp) from IAM systems
count of access grants with no linked request ID
remote access/VPN request logs referencing remote work
emergency/exception request and exception approval logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between access request/ticket ID and provisioning execution in IAM/change logs, with immutable timestamps and identities.
Strong: continuous system logs showing 100% of grants reference a request ID and approval record.
Weak: ad-hoc screenshots, periodic manual spreadsheets, or retrospective attestations that do not link request IDs to provisioning events.
Weak: sample-based or annual evidence without continuous logs, which does not demonstrate prevention of unauthorized grants in real time.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'Access requests required' implies a gating/prevention step before access is granted, so the control prevents unauthorized grants by requiring requests and approvals.

IAC-9.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires evidence that every access grant originates from an approved request (linkage between request ID and provisioning action), unlike sibling controls that may focus on periodic access reviews or detection of unauthorized access after the fact.

IAC-10.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of network systems that require and enforce unique per-user authentication (no shared or default accounts) and track counts of shared/default credentials and unauthenticated/anonymous access events to demonstrate enforcement.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control focused on coverage/enforcement: auditors need coverage percentages (devices with unique auth vs. total) as primary evidence, with counts of shared accounts and rates of unauthorized/anonymous access as secondary operational signals.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of network devices and systems (routers, switches, firewalls, networked appliances, servers providing network services, VPN concentrators) that have per-user/unique authentication configured and enforced (no shared/local generic accounts), plus counts of identified shared/default accounts and number of authentication events attributable to anonymous/shared accounts over the audit period.
data_signals: asset inventory with device type and unique device identifier
authentication configuration records per device (authentication backend: local vs. RADIUS/LDAP/AD binding)
per-device account listings annotated with account_type or tag (individual, service, shared, default)
list of accounts with last_login and login source (to attribute activity to unique users)
VPN/remote-access authentication logs showing user identifiers per session
MFA/enrollment status per user mapped to device access
configuration scan results indicating presence of default credentials or shared accounts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated configuration scans and inventory showing 100% of devices bound to centralized directory with per-user authentication, plus continuous auth logs tying every session to an individual user.
Strong: absence of devices with local/shared/default accounts in configuration scan results.
Weak: point-in-time screenshots of device settings or yearly attestation without supporting logs.
Weak: manual lists of accounts without linkage to device-level configuration or authentication logs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name emphasizes 'Unique' authentication and 'enforced', indicating prevention of shared/default credentials before misuse occurs.

IAC-10.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires enforcement of unique per-user authentication on network systems (coverage/absence-of-shared-accounts measurement) rather than detection or remediation activities such as logging anomalies or incident response metrics.

IAC-11.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of active user accounts that comply with the organization's enforced password policy (policy object present and account attributes conform), the count/rate of authentication events blocked or rejected due to password policy, and the number and status of approved exceptions or waivers.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control: auditors need coverage/percent-compliant (primary) plus counts of enforcement events and rates of non-compliance/remediation over time to show effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate percentage of active accounts whose stored attributes meet the configured password policy (e.g., password age within max_age, presence of policy object for the identity store), count of authentication attempts rejected for password-policy violations, number of account lockouts triggered by policy thresholds, and count of approved exceptions. Include timestamps so measurements can be trended and compared to policy thresholds.
data_signals: directory/IdP password policy configuration objects (min_length, max_age, lockout_threshold) as recorded in configuration store
user account password metadata: last_password_change timestamp and password_age per account
count of active user accounts flagged non-compliant with current password policy
authentication logs showing password rejection events and reasons (policy_violation, complexity_failure)
account lockout events and lockout timestamps
approved password policy exception/waiver records with approver and expiry
password reset request and completion logs (self-service reset timestamps)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated directory/IdP configuration export showing policy settings plus continuous auth logs with enforcement reason codes and low percentage of non-compliant accounts
Strong: time-series of percent-compliant trending against policy_threshold and documented approved exceptions
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots of policy settings, or ad-hoc manual attestations without logs
Weak: absence of enforcement reason codes in auth logs (makes it unclear whether policy blocked events)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'policy enforced' indicates a preventive control that blocks or restricts weak/invalid passwords before access is granted.

IAC-11.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on enforcement and account-level compliance (percent compliant, enforcement event counts, exceptions) rather than detection or post-incident remediation metrics used by sibling controls.

IAC-12.measurement_goal: Measure enforcement coverage and operational outcome of MFA for all remote access methods: percentage of remote authentication attempts protected by MFA, count of remote-capable accounts/services with MFA disabled, and rate of MFA failures or bypasses against defined full-coverage expectations.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive coverage control (enforcement); auditors will want percent coverage (MFA applied to remote auths). Counts and rates (e.g., number of bypasses, MFA failure rate) are useful secondary signals to detect gaps or operational impact.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percentage of remote authentication events where an MFA challenge was required and successful (MFA_challenged_and_successful / total_remote_authentications) per remote access system and in aggregate; produce a list/count of user accounts, service accounts, and applications that permit remote access but do not have MFA enforced; and count/track MFA bypass or exception records and failed MFA attempts over time.
data_signals: authentication event logs with MFA challenge flag and outcome (success/failure)
timestamps and source (IP/device) for remote authentication events
MFA configuration/enforcement settings for remote access systems (VPN, SSO, cloud console, remote desktop gateways)
user and service-account MFA enrollment and status
list of approved MFA exceptions or bypass tokens and their expiration
count of remote-capable accounts that have MFA disabled
alerts or incidents referencing unauthorized remote access or MFA bypass attempts
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous authentication logs with per-event MFA flag plus automated policy/configuration evidence showing MFA enforcement across all remote access systems
Strong: conditional access/SSO policy exports showing MFA required for remote access and exceptions tracked with expirations
Weak: one-time attestations, manual spreadsheets, or annual statements of enforcement without per-event logs
Weak: screenshots of configuration pages without machine-readable policy exports or event logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Enforced' in the control name indicates the control's purpose is to prevent unauthorized access by requiring MFA for remote access.

IAC-12.differentiation_note: Focuses specifically on enforcement and coverage of MFA for remote-access channels (per-event enforcement and exceptions tracking), rather than general MFA enrollment or on-premise access controls.

IAC-13.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of remote access connections to corporate systems that use approved, enforced encryption (approved TLS/VPN cipher suites) and count/identify any remote sessions or endpoints that bypass or fail that enforcement policy.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive enforcement control so coverage/percentage metrics (percent of sessions compliant) are most useful; counts of non‑compliant sessions and rates of occurrences are secondary to quantify incidents and trends.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute percent of remote access sessions to corporate resources that used approved encryption protocols (e.g., TLS 1.2+, approved cipher suites, or corporate VPN) during the reporting period, and list/count sessions that negotiated weak/no encryption. Also measure percent of managed endpoints configured to require the corporate encrypted remote access channel (VPN/managed TLS client) and count policy exceptions.
data_signals: VPN connection logs indicating session start/end, user, source IP, cipher suite and TLS version
Load balancer / TLS termination logs with TLS version and negotiated cipher per session
Remote access session metadata (protocol type, encryption flag, timestamp, destination resource)
Network device / VPN configuration records showing 'require-encryption' or allowed-ciphers settings
Endpoint compliance reports showing presence/configuration of corporate VPN client or enforced VPN profile
Inventory of policy exceptions / approved bypasses for encrypted remote access
Count of sessions flagged as unencrypted or using deprecated ciphers during the period
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous automated VPN/TLS session logs and device configuration snapshots demonstrating enforcement and zero (or policy-defined) unencrypted sessions.
Strong: centralized endpoint compliance records showing enforced VPN client/profile installation and no active exceptions.
Weak: single-point-in-time attestations or screenshots of VPN settings without session logs, or annual manual statements without session-level evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'enforced' in the control name indicates a preventive configuration/policy meant to stop non‑encrypted remote access before it occurs.

IAC-13.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of encryption enforcement for remote access sessions (session-level encryption/cipher evidence and enforcement configuration), distinguishing it from sibling controls that may focus on authentication, access reviews, or endpoint hardening.

IAO-1.measurement_goal: Measure the completion, scope coverage, and remediation follow-up of scheduled control self-assessments (number completed per cycle, percent of organizational units/systems assessed, and count/SLA-compliance of findings remediated) to demonstrate assessments are conducted and issues are tracked to closure.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Self-assessments are primarily evidenced by counts (assessments completed, findings logged, remediation tickets created); percentages (coverage, completion rate) and trends (open findings over time) are useful supplements to show coverage and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of scheduled self-assessments vs completed per assessment cycle; percentage coverage of systems/sites and business units (including remote work sites) included in each assessment; number of findings by severity and corresponding remediation tickets with open/close timestamps to measure SLA compliance and time-to-remediate.
data_signals: self-assessment schedule / calendar entries with owner and required frequency
completed self-assessment reports with timestamps and assessed scope (systems, sites, business units)
inventory of assessed systems/sites indicating inclusion of remote work sites
finding records from assessments (finding id, severity, description)
remediation ticket ids linked to findings with open and close timestamps
SLA or policy thresholds for assessment frequency and remediation timelines
attestation or sign-off records from control owners for each completed assessment
coverage percentage by unit/site (assessed assets / total assets) per cycle
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated schedule records + timestamped assessment reports + linked remediation tickets showing closure within policy SLA and coverage including remote work sites.
Moderate: dated assessment reports without automated scheduling or partial linkage to remediation tickets.
Weak: anecdotal or unsigned statements that assessments were 'conducted' or a one-off memo without scope, timestamps, or remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name ('Control self-assessments conducted') indicates periodic assessment activity intended to detect control gaps, so it functions primarily as a detective control.

IAO-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on conducting and evidencing self-assessments (completion, scope, and remediation tracking) rather than active prevention or technical detection — measurement must emphasize assessment cadence and coverage (including remote work sites) rather than runtime telemetry.

IAO-2.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that authorized penetration tests are performed against in-scope systems on a scheduled or risk-driven cadence, that findings are recorded with severity, and that remediation or risk-acceptance is tracked to closure (including retest verification). An auditor would expect counts of tests by environment, coverage of in-scope assets, distribution of findings by severity, and remediation/verification rates and times against policy requirements.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Penetration testing is primarily a detective activity where auditors expect counts (number of tests, findings). Secondary metrics (percentages for remediation/coverage, trends for improvement, distribution of severities) capture scope and remediation effectiveness as required by the control.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of penetration tests executed per environment/application per reporting period; list and count of findings grouped by severity (e.g., critical/high/medium/low) with CVSS/CWE identifiers where available; remediation ticket lifecycle data (created timestamp, remediation completed timestamp, verification/retest timestamp) tied to each finding; scope coverage listing (assets/IPs/components) for each test and evidence of authorization/engagement.
data_signals: penetration test report identifiers and execution timestamps
test scope documents (asset/IP list, application names, environment tags)
findings table with severity (critical/high/medium/low), CVSS score, CWE/CVE references
remediation ticket IDs with created/updated/closed timestamps and assigned owner
retest or verification report IDs and timestamps showing finding closure
engagement/authorization letter or statement of work for each test
policy-defined testing frequency or schedule (policy_threshold) and evidence of adherence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: formal penetration test reports plus linked remediation tickets and verified retest evidence demonstrating closure for each finding.
Strong: documented scope and engagement records showing authorized, in-scope testing of production and remote-access interfaces.
Weak: single point-in-time attestations or unsigned/undated summary emails without remediation ticket linkage or retest verification.
Weak: only vulnerability scan results without an authorized penetration test report when control requires a pen test.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Penetration testing performed' indicates an activity that identifies vulnerabilities and weaknesses (detective) rather than preventing or correcting them directly.

IAO-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on periodic authorized external/internal penetration tests and tracking of their findings and retests, unlike sibling controls that might focus on continuous vulnerability scanning or configuration compliance; measurement must emphasize formal reports, scope coverage, and remediation verification rather than only automated scan coverage.

IRO-1.measurement_goal: Measure the frequency and recency of incident response plan tests (exercises) and the remediation follow‑through: count of tests executed per 12‑month period, time since last test, percent of exercise findings remediated within defined remediation SLAs, and participation by required roles to demonstrate the plan is exercised at least annually.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: The control requires measuring tests over a time window (e.g., tests per 12 months) and remediation velocity; rate captures frequency (tests/year, remediation rate) while counts and percentages show volume and coverage and trends show recurrence and improvements.
