evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of incident response plan tests executed in the last 12 months, date of most recent test, test type (tabletop/functional/live), list of roles/participants present, existence of after-action reports listing findings, number and IDs of remediation tickets created from findings, and percent of those remediation items closed within the policy SLA (e.g., X days).
data_signals: incident response exercise execution timestamps (dates of exercises)
exercise type and scope metadata (tabletop, functional, live; systems covered)
attendance rosters / participant role lists for each exercise
after-action reports / exercise findings and severity classifications
remediation ticket IDs created from exercise findings and their creation/closure timestamps
time since last exercise (days)
policy or schedule showing required annual test cadence
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: dated exercise records + signed attendance rosters + detailed after‑action reports + linked remediation tickets showing closure within SLA.
Moderate: exercise reports without participant lists or unresolved remediation items.
Weak: a standalone dated plan document stating 'annual tests' but no execution records or remediation evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires testing the IR plan and tracking remediation (improvements after exercises), which aligns with corrective controls that drive fixes and process improvement.

IRO-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on exercising and validating the incident response plan on a recurring (annual) cadence and tracking remediation from tests, rather than on maintaining the plan document or on detection tooling — measurement must demonstrate execution and follow‑through rather than just existence of documentation.

IRO-2.measurement_goal: Measure the presence and maintenance of the incident response policy and supporting governance artifacts: percent of required IR policy elements that are documented and approved (policy document, assigned owners/roles, contact/escalation list, forensic procedures, cyber insurance evidence) and operationalized (recent review, tabletop exercises, training acknowledgements). Also measure counts of exercises and outstanding policy-related action items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive governance control (existence/coverage of policy elements) so percent coverage/completion is the most useful; counts of exercises and trend of reviews/remediations are useful secondaries to show operationalization over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Calculate the percentage of mandatory IR policy artifacts that exist and are approved (policy document with version & approval timestamp, assigned owners for each IR role, published contact/escalation list, documented forensic procedures, evidence of cyber insurance). Also measure number of tabletop exercises in the last 12 months, number of staff who have acknowledged IR training, and count of open action items from AARs by age.
data_signals: incident response policy document version and approval timestamp
IR roles/owners roster (RACI or assigned responsibilities) with timestamps
published incident contact and escalation list with last update timestamp
forensic procedures document and evidence of forensic readiness tests
cyber insurance policy copy and coverage dates (certificate of insurance)
tabletop/exercise schedule entries and after-action reports with remediation items
training completion or acknowledgement records for staff assigned IR responsibilities
open action items from IR reviews / AARs with creation and closure timestamps
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: versioned, signed policy documents with approval timestamps, automated attestations or acknowledgements, scheduled tabletop exercises and AARs with closed remediation items, current contact list with last-modified dates, and an on-file cyber insurance certificate.
Weak: undated or unsigned policy drafts, oral confirmations of roles, single historical exercise report without follow-up, or absence of documented forensic procedures.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Incident response policies established' indicates preventative governance — establishing policies and roles to avoid delays or ineffective response.

IRO-2.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the existence, approval, assignment and maintenance of IR policy artifacts (contacts, roles, forensic procedures, insurance) rather than on detection or incident-level metrics like time-to-contain; its measurements emphasize policy coverage, exercise cadence, and documented ownership.

IRO-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage and count of incidents where documented incident management procedures (notifications, forensic evidence collection, containment, remediation and reporting) were executed on time, plus timeliness metrics (time-to-triage, time-to-containment, time-to-closure) and presence of required artifacts (contact notifications, chain-of-custody, post-incident review).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about verifying whether procedures were followed (a detection/compliance outcome) so a rate (adherence rate or notification success rate) is most useful; counts of incidents, trending adherence over time and percentage of incidents meeting time/SLA thresholds are complementary.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each incident record, capture timestamps for key procedure steps (created, triage, containment, eradication, recovery, closure), boolean flags or checklist items showing whether required actions occurred (contact notifications sent, forensic image taken, chain-of-custody recorded, remediation tasks created/completed), and links to supporting artifacts (notification receipts, forensic logs, post-incident review). Calculate adherence rate = incidents with all required procedure steps completed / total incidents, plus median and SLA-violation counts for time-to-containment and time-to-closure.
data_signals: incident ticket lifecycle timestamps (created, triage, containment, eradication, recovery, closure)
incident checklist fields (contacts notified, forensics initiated, evidence preserved, remediation created)
notification delivery receipts / escalation log entries (email/SMS/phone) with timestamps and recipient identifiers
forensic evidence collection logs (disk/image capture timestamps, hash values, chain-of-custody entries)
post-incident review reports and root-cause entries (open/closed status)
remediation action items with assignment, status, and remediation completion timestamps
incident playbook or procedure version referenced in ticket
cyber insurance policy existence/identifier (policy present) and any claim-notification records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated incident tickets with structured checklist fields, immutable timestamps, notification delivery receipts, and chain-of-custody logs tied to each incident.
Moderate: centralized ticket records with manual attachments (post-incident reports) but missing delivery receipts or automated timestamps.
Weak: ad-hoc email threads, undocumented verbal notifications, or annual summaries without per-incident artifacts; these do not prove procedures were followed for each incident.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control verifies that incident management procedures were followed (i.e., checking execution and evidence after incidents), which is a detective/compliance verification activity.

IRO-3.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on measuring procedural adherence, notifications, forensic evidence and remediation tracking per incident (adherence rates and timeliness), rather than preventive activities like vulnerability scanning or ongoing configuration hardening.

MDM-1.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and enforcement of the organization's MDM: percentage of corporate mobile devices enrolled in the MDM, percentage of enrolled devices meeting required compliance policies (encryption, passcode, OS patching), count of unmanaged devices accessing corporate resources, and volume of enforcement actions (remote wipes/quarantines) over time.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (MDM used to enforce policy) so coverage and compliance are best expressed as percentages; counts and rates (unmanaged devices, enforcement actions, enrollment velocity) are useful secondary measures to show scale and activity and trends over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: A data set mapping device identifiers to MDM enrollment status and compliance attributes (enrollment timestamp, OS version, encryption enabled, passcode set, jailbreak/root detection), plus logs of enforcement actions and records of devices attempting access while unmanaged. Calculate: % of corporate mobile devices enrolled, % of enrolled devices that pass all required compliance checks, count of unmanaged devices accessing corporate services, and mean time-to-enroll.
data_signals: device inventory with MDM enrollment status (device_id, user, ownership_type)
MDM enrollment timestamps and enrollment source
device compliance checks/results (OS version, encryption enabled, passcode status, jailbreak/root flag)
MDM policy push success/failure logs and timestamped policy compliance reports
authentication/access logs with device identifiers for VPN, email, cloud services
count of unmanaged or un-enrolled devices attempting or succeeding in accessing corporate resources
remote wipe / quarantine / conditional access enforcement action logs with timestamps
time-to-enroll (time between device first seen and MDM enrollment)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous MDM telemetry (enrollment events, policy compliance reports, enforcement logs) with timestamps is strong evidence
Automated correlation between device inventory and access logs (showing unmanaged device access attempts) strengthens evidence
Point-in-time manual attestations or spreadsheets without enrollment timestamps are weak evidence
Sparse or infrequent snapshots (e.g., annual reports) are weak compared to continuous MDM logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'MDM system utilized' implies a preventive control to enforce secure device configuration and block insecure devices before they access corporate systems.

MDM-1.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about MDM deployment, enrollment coverage, and policy enforcement on mobile endpoints (coverage and compliance percentages), not about downstream detection or incident remediation — measurement must focus on enrollment and enforcement telemetry rather than incident alerts. (Control description was minimal; assumptions made from the control name and associated risks.)

MON-1.measurement_goal: Measure continuous IDS detection activity and coverage: count of IDS alerts by severity and signature, percent of in‑scope network segments and remote endpoints covered by IDS sensors, mean time to acknowledge/triage IDS alerts, and number of IDS alerts escalated to confirmed incidents or breaches.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control, raw event counts (alerts) best show IDS activity and detection coverage; rates (alerts per asset/time), coverage % and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show effectiveness and improvement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Continuous IDS alert activity and sensor deployment coverage. Specifically: count of IDS alerts per sensor and per severity over time (with signature ID, source/destination IP, asset ID), inventory of deployed IDS sensors mapped to network segments and remote sites, timestamps for alert generation and alert acknowledgement/triage, IDS rule/signature version and last update, and linkage of alerts to incident/ticket IDs (including confirmed breach correlation).
data_signals: IDS alert logs: alert_id, timestamp, sensor_id, signature_id, severity, source_ip, dest_ip, asset_id
IDS sensor inventory and deployment status by network segment and remote work site
IDS rule/signature package version and last update timestamp
alert acknowledgement/triage timestamps and analyst_id (time to acknowledge/triage)
alert → incident/ticket linkage (ticket_id, escalation timestamp, confirmed_breach flag)
false_positive/false_negative counts and tuning/change records
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Continuous machine‑readable IDS alert logs with full fields (sensor_id, signature_id, timestamps) plus an up‑to‑date sensor inventory and automated linkage of alerts to incident tickets.
Strong: Regular automated signature/rule update records and demonstrable coverage mapping of sensors to all in‑scope network segments and remote endpoints.
Weak: Point‑in‑time screenshots of alerts, manual summaries, or annual reports without raw alert logs or sensor deployment evidence.
Weak: Alert counts without acknowledgement timestamps or without mapping to assets and incidents (cannot demonstrate response or coverage).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Intrusion detection system utilized' describes a monitoring/detection capability designed to detect intrusions, which is a detective control.

MON-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on continuous intrusion detection activity and coverage (alerts, sensor deployment, rule updates, and alert→incident linkage), rather than broader logging or preventive network controls; measurement must emphasize continuous alert streams and coverage mapping. (Note: control description text was minimal, so keywords and risks were used to expand coverage emphasis.)

MON-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational effectiveness of log management by reporting the count of systems and sources forwarding logs to a centralized store, the percentage meeting required retention/configuration policy, and counts of missing or failed log deliveries and log-derived alerts indicating operational/security issues.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Log management is primarily detective — auditors need event and source counts (ingestion, missing sources, alerts). Percentage is useful for coverage/retention compliance and trend for monitoring improvements or regressions.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of distinct log sources (hosts, network devices, apps) actively ingesting logs to the centralized logging system; percentage of those sources configured to meet retention and schema/policy requirements; count and timestamps of log ingestion failures or gaps; count of correlation/alerting events generated from logs. Include per-source retention duration, ingestion latency, and last-received timestamp.
data_signals: centralized log ingestion events (timestamp, source_id, event_count)
inventory of onboarded log sources with last_received_timestamp
log forwarding/configuration status per host (enabled/disabled, agent version)
log retention period recorded per source and retention enforcement flags
log ingestion latency metrics (event timestamp vs. ingestion timestamp)
count of log ingestion failures or rejected batches with error codes
alerts generated by log correlation or SIEM rules (alert_id, rule_id, timestamp)
hashes/integrity/audit trail entries for archived logs
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous automated ingestion logs and per-source last_received_timestamp are strong evidence;
centralized SIEM/ELK index counts and immutable archived log storage strengthen evidence;
Point-in-time screenshots or ad-hoc manual log exports are weak evidence;
Frequent successful ingestion, low ingestion latency, and documented retention enforcement indicate high strength; large counts of missing sources or ingestion failures indicate weak evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title implies use of logging to observe events and detect issues, which is primarily detective in nature.

MON-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of log-source coverage, ingestion health, retention enforcement, and alert counts — unlike sibling controls focused on access or vulnerability management, its unique need is continuous ingestion/retention and source-onboarding metrics; note: control description was empty so keywords were supplemented from associated risk texts.

MON-4.measurement_goal: Measure monitoring coverage (percentage of infrastructure with active monitoring agents), the rate of performance incidents (alerts) per service per time period, and mean time to detect and resolve performance degradations compared to policy thresholds.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a detective monitoring control — auditors need rates of performance incidents and detection velocity; coverage and compliance are best expressed as percentages and counts, and trending shows improvement or regressions over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Report the percent of infrastructure components (hosts, VMs, containers, network devices) with active monitoring agents and data ingestion, the rate of performance alerts (per service/application) by severity per day/week, average latency/response-time breaching events, and mean time to detect and mean time to resolve those events versus policy thresholds.
data_signals: monitoring agent heartbeat/registration logs (host/container IDs, last-seen timestamp)
resource utilization metrics (CPU, memory, disk I/O) per host with timestamps
service response-time and latency time-series (p95, p99) per application
performance alert records (alert id, severity, rule id, trigger timestamp, affected host/service)
incident/ticket timestamps for detection and resolution (created, acknowledged, resolved)
synthetic transaction results and failure counts
list of covered infrastructure assets vs. asset inventory (coverage mapping)
SLA or policy thresholds for performance (threshold values, window, severity mapping)
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous metric time-series and alert logs with agent heartbeats (strong) vs. sporadic manual reports or single-point screenshots (weak)
Correlation of alerts to incident tickets and closure timestamps (strong) vs. alerts with no ticketing evidence (weak)
High coverage percentage of assets with active agents and ingestion (strong) vs. gaps between asset inventory and monitored assets (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name specifies monitoring of infrastructure performance, which is primarily a detective activity to identify performance degradations and operational impact.

MON-4.differentiation_note: Unlike vulnerability or access controls, this control focuses on continuous performance telemetry and alerting coverage (agent heartbeats, latency, utilization) rather than patching or access review metrics.

NET-1.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of data transmissions and external/internal connections that are encrypted in transit (by TLS/VPN/other approved transport encryption), the count of unencrypted or weakly-encrypted sessions, and certificate/cipher compliance for those encrypted sessions.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires measuring coverage (percent of sessions/flows encrypted). Counts of unencrypted sessions and rates/trends of failures are useful secondary signals to track incidents and remediation over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the ratio of encrypted sessions to total sessions per service, per vendor connection, and per remote-worker connection (e.g., encrypted_sessions / total_sessions) over a rolling period; count of sessions using deprecated TLS versions or weak ciphers; count of connections using explicitly unencrypted protocols; and certificate validity/expiry status for endpoints handling sensitive data.
data_signals: TLS/HTTPS handshake logs with TLS version and cipher suite
network flow logs (NetFlow/IPFIX) indicating encryption flag or port/protocol
proxy/load balancer access logs showing HTTP->HTTPS redirects and enforcement
VPN connection/session logs for remote workers
counts of connections using unencrypted protocols (e.g., HTTP, FTP, plain TCP) by source/destination
certificate inventory and expiration/validation records (issuer, expiry, SANs)
list of vendor integration endpoints and their encryption enforcement settings (e.g., TLS required)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous network/proxy/flow telemetry showing TLS handshakes, cipher suites, and session counts correlated to asset/service inventory; automated alerts for unencrypted or weakly-encrypted sessions; certificate management system records.
Weak: one-time configuration screenshots, annual test reports, or manual attestations without supporting session logs and certificate telemetry.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Data transmission encrypted' describes a preventative measure that stops breaches in transit by enforcing encryption.

NET-1.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about in-transit encryption coverage (network/TLS/VPN telemetry and certificate posture) rather than endpoint or at-rest encryption, requiring continuous network-level and certificate signals rather than solely configuration snapshots.

NET-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and enforcement of network segmentation for assets that store or process customer data: the percentage of customer-data assets placed in isolated segments, the count of permitted cross-segment connections to those segments, and the presence of approved exceptions with expiration.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage metrics (percentage of customer-data assets correctly segmented). Counts of cross-segment rules and distributions of flow events supplement coverage; comparisons to policy/full segmentation baselines verify adequacy.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (1) percentage of assets that store/process customer data mapped to a segmented (isolated) network zone; (2) count of active firewall/ACL rules that permit inbound or lateral access to those zones from untrusted segments or vendor networks; (3) number of approved segmentation exceptions and their expiry status; (4) volume of blocked cross-segment access attempts against those zones within a reporting window.
data_signals: asset-to-network-zone mapping with sensitivity tag (customer data flag)
network zone inventory and segmentation topology diagrams
firewall and ACL rule listings with source/destination/ports and rule status
microsegmentation policy/rules (for SDN/container environments)
network flow logs (NetFlow/IPFIX) showing cross-zone flows with source/destination IPs and bytes
NGFW/IDS blocked cross-segment connection events and timestamps
VPN/remote-access and vendor session logs with destination zone and user/vendor identifier
segmentation exception tickets/approvals with expiration dates
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous enforcement logs (NGFW/IDS) showing blocked/allowed cross-zone attempts, up-to-date asset-to-zone inventory, and automated mapping of customer-data assets to isolated zones
Moderate: recent exports of firewall/ACL configurations and flow logs with correlation to asset inventory
Weak: static network diagrams or one-time architecture documents without recent configuration or flow evidence; manual attestations without logs

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states segmentation is implemented "to prevent unauthorized access," indicating a preventive control aimed at reducing attack surface before an event.

NET-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires measurement of segmentation coverage and enforcement for customer-data assets (asset-to-zone mapping and cross-segment rule counts), unlike sibling controls that might focus on perimeter protections or encryption in transit.

NET-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percent of production network firewall devices and rulebases that have a documented review within the required review period, the count of high-risk/overly-permissive rules found during reviews, and the remediation/exception closure rate for review findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control (periodic reviews) so auditors expect coverage/percentage metrics (e.g., % of firewalls reviewed on schedule). Counts of risky rules and remediation rates are useful secondary metrics to show scope and velocity of fix activity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: For each firewall asset measure the timestamp of the last documented review, the reviewer identity, number of risky rules identified in that review (e.g., 0.0.0.0/0, ANY-ANY opens), whether each finding has remediation or an approved exception, and time-to-remediation for each finding compared to policy-defined review/remediation timelines.
data_signals: firewall device inventory (asset ID, location, owner, managed-by/vendor flag)
timestamped firewall rule-set exports (configuration snapshots) with rule identifiers
firewall rule change logs (timestamp, author, change diff, source of change)
formal review reports/attestations (reviewer name, date, findings, approvals)
list of rules flagged as 'wide-open' or 'allow any' (IP:port combinations, protocol)
exception/compensating control approvals with expiry dates
remediation actions and closure timestamps for review findings
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped rule-set exports and change histories stored in configuration management or version control plus signed review reports and remediation ticket links
Strong: consistent periodic review entries for each firewall asset showing reviewer and actions
Weak: single point-in-time screenshots or unsigned notes without change logs
Weak: undocumented exceptions or lack of remediation timestamps

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title 'Network firewalls reviewed' indicates proactive periodic reviews to prevent misconfiguration and breaches, which is preventive in nature.

NET-3.differentiation_note: This control is specifically about periodic review and evidence of firewall rulebase/configuration audits (timestamps, reviewer attestations, rule findings) rather than continuous network monitoring or incident detection; measurement must emphasize rule exports, review artifacts, and remediation tracking. (Control description is limited; measurement assumptions are based on the control name and associated risk text.)

NET-4.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion of network zones and assets protected by configured firewalls that enforce deny/allow policies per organizational policy, and count/configure permissive rules or exceptions (including vendor/remote access) that could permit unauthorized access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage and compliance percentages (e.g., percent of network segments with approved firewall policies). Counts and rates (e.g., number of permissive rules, rate of emergency rule additions) supplement coverage to show exposure and change velocity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of network segments and cloud subnets with an active, approved firewall policy applied; count of firewall rules that allow broad or unrestricted access and count of active approved exceptions (vendor or remote access) with expiration/approval metadata; timestamps of last configuration review and change request IDs to show ongoing governance.
data_signals: firewall configuration exports (rule sets) with device ID and timestamp
mapping of network segments/subnets to firewall device or security group
list of firewall rules flagged as permissive (e.g., wide CIDR or any-port allowances) with creation/modification timestamps
firewall change management records (change request ID, approver, timestamp)
approved exception/temporary-access records for vendor or remote access with expiry
firewall allow/deny traffic logs showing denied attempts and allowed flows by source/destination
firewall device software/firmware version and patch status
configuration compliance scan results against approved baseline
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated, versioned firewall configuration exports and continuous compliance scan results are strong evidence.
Change-management records linked to configuration diffs and approvals strengthen evidence.
Frequent firewall logs showing denied unauthorized attempts plus no recent successful unauthorized flows indicate effective operation.
Point-in-time screenshots or undocumented manual notes are weak evidence; missing exception approval/expiry data weakens evidence.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states the company 'uses firewalls and configures them to prevent unauthorized access', indicating a preventive configuration control.

NET-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses on firewall coverage and rule/configuration quality (coverage, permissive rules, approved exceptions) rather than detection or remediation metrics used by sibling controls like IDS logging or patch remediation.

NET-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of network devices and host systems that conform to the approved network/system hardening standard (by version) and track exceptions and remediation time so an auditor can see baseline application coverage and outstanding non‑compliant items.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive control (standards maintained) where auditors need coverage rates (percentage compliant). Counts of noncompliant items, trends over time, and configuration compliance scores are useful secondary measures to show scope and progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of assets (network devices, servers, endpoints, cloud instances) with an applied hardening profile matching the approved standard version; counts and details of exception records (with justification and expiry); timestamps of last compliance scan and last hardening change; remediation time for noncompliant findings.
data_signals: approved hardening standard document metadata (version, approval date)
asset inventory with device type, hostname, environment, and criticality
configuration/compliance scan results per asset (benchmark score, pass/fail per control)
compliance status percentage by device category (hosts, network devices, cloud instances)
list of configuration exceptions with ticket id, owner, justification, expiry date
change management records showing hardening-related changes (ticket id, applied timestamp)
last successful hardening/scan timestamp per asset and scan failure reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated configuration compliance scan results covering the full asset inventory (strong) vs. one-off manual checklists or screenshots (weak).
Versioned/approved hardening standard stored in a controlled repository and referenced by scans (strong) vs. undocumented ad-hoc settings (weak).
Continuous or regular scheduled scans with low scan-failure rate and reconciled exceptions (strong) vs. infrequent scans and many aged exceptions (weak).
Change tickets showing hardening applied with timestamps (strong) vs. no change history (weak).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'standards maintained' indicates a preventive configuration and hardening requirement intended to stop insecure configurations before incidents occur.

NET-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on maintaining and applying hardening standards and measuring configuration compliance (coverage, exceptions, versioning), unlike sibling controls that might focus on detection, patching cadence, or incident response metrics.

OPS-1.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and execution of vulnerability scanning and the presence and health of continuous system monitoring: percent of assets with monitoring agents installed, frequency and success rate of vulnerability scans, counts of intrusion/malware alerts, and mean time to remediate high-severity findings.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Because the control emphasizes system monitoring (a detective function) and vulnerability processes, event and alert counts (detections) are primary for proving monitoring is operating; coverage percentages and trends are secondary to show scope and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete indicators showing monitoring and vulnerability procedures are active and effective: (1) inventory of assets with monitoring agents and their last heartbeat timestamp, (2) vulnerability scan execution records per asset (timestamp, success/failure), (3) open vulnerability counts broken down by severity and remediation ticket timestamps, (4) counts of intrusion/malware alerts and log ingestion rates to centralized logging.
data_signals: asset inventory with monitoring agent installed flag and last-checkin timestamp
vulnerability scan execution timestamps and scan success/failure status
open vulnerability count by severity (per asset) and remediation ticket creation/closure timestamps
intrusion/malware alert counts and alert timestamps from IDS/AV/EDR
log ingestion rate (events/min) and last-received log timestamp to centralized SIEM
system uptime and capacity metrics (CPU/memory/disk) and incidents of system unavailability
log integrity / retention records and evidence of log collection for critical systems
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous agent heartbeats + uninterrupted log ingestion into a centralized SIEM, automated vulnerability scan logs with success statuses, and linked remediation tickets with closure timestamps.
Weak: one-off manual scan reports, intermittent screenshots of monitoring dashboards, or sample-based checks that do not show ongoing coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title emphasizes 'system monitoring', which is primarily a detective activity (detecting intrusions, malware, availability issues).

OPS-1.differentiation_note: This control uniquely combines vulnerability management procedures with continuous system monitoring — measurement must include both agent/scan coverage (percentage) and detection event volumes (counts), whereas sibling controls would focus on only one of those areas.

PES-1.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and compliance of established physical access processes across controlled facilities (percentage of access points and assets covered), count of unauthorized physical access attempts and exceptions, and timeliness of access provisioning/deprovisioning and physical-incident responses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This preventive control requires demonstrating coverage and compliance (best expressed as percentages or coverage scores); counts of incidents/attempts and trending over time are useful secondary evidence of effectiveness and change.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of controlled facilities and access points covered by documented physical access processes (badge/entry/visitor procedures), count and rate of unauthorized access attempts and physical security incidents, percentage of access provisioning/deprovisioning actions completed within policy timeframes, and uptime/maintenance evidence for critical facility equipment supporting availability.
data_signals: physical access / entry control process documents (established procedures)
access provisioning and deprovisioning records with timestamps
access event logs (badge/entry events) with user/door/timestamp
recorded unauthorized access attempts or access exception approvals
visitor sign-in / escort records and timestamps
physical-incident and breach reports (incident timestamps and resolution)
facility equipment maintenance and test logs (UPS/generator/other environmental equipment)
records of access reviews and completion status
evidence_strength_indicators: Continuous, tamper-evident access event logs correlated with provisioning records are strong evidence;
automated timestamps showing deprovisioning when employees leave are strong;
periodic manual sign-in sheets or one-off facility walk-through notes are weak;
complete coverage across all controlled facilities and documented maintenance logs for critical equipment strengthen evidence vs partial or missing coverage.

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'Processes established' and focus on preventing unauthorized access and equipment-related availability issues indicate this is primarily a preventive control.

PES-1.differentiation_note: Unlike logical access controls, this control focuses on physical entry/process coverage and facility resilience (maintenance and provisioning/deprovisioning), so measurement must include physical event logs, visitor records, and equipment maintenance evidence rather than only digital authentication metrics.

PES-2.measurement_goal: Measure the proportion and count of data centers that have completed an authorized physical access review within the last 12 months, the count of review findings (unauthorized access or exceptions), and the count and timeliness of follow-up remediation actions tied to those reviews.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a detective control the primary evidence is occurrence counts (number of reviews completed, findings discovered, remediation tickets opened/closed). Percentage coverage and trends over review cycles are useful secondary metrics to show coverage and recurring compliance.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of completed access review events per data center per 12-month period (review_id, data_center_id, review_date, reviewer_id), percentage of total data centers reviewed within the last 12 months, count of findings per review (unauthorized access, exceptions), and remediation ticket counts and closure timestamps linked to review findings.
data_signals: data center inventory (site id, location, owner, criticality)
access review schedule and completion records (review_id, data_center_id, review_date, reviewer_id, sign-off)
review findings / exception logs (finding id, type: unauthorized access, exception, physical control deficiency)
remediation tickets created from review findings (ticket id, created_at, closed_at, resolution)
list of individuals with physical access grants per data center and grant timestamps
access-control event logs / badge entry logs correlated by data center and user (last access date)
overdue review flags or missed review records (planned vs completed review dates)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: Signed/completed review records with reviewer identity and timestamps, remediation tickets linked to specific findings with closed_at timestamps, and cross-correlation to access grant lists and badge logs.
Weak: Verbal attestations, undated spreadsheets, or single annual summary emails without reviewer sign-off or linked remediation evidence.
Strong: Coverage showing 100% of data centers reviewed within the last 12 months and decreasing trend in findings year-over-year; Weak: one-off review for a subset of sites only.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires periodic reviews ('reviews access ... at least annually'), which is detective in nature because it identifies issues after-the-fact rather than preventing them.

PES-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets periodic physical access reviews for data centers (physical-site coverage and review artifacts) rather than logical access controls or continuous monitoring; measurement must emphasize site-level review completion, findings, and remediation linkage.

PES-3.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of visitor events that fully comply with the visitor procedure (signed-in with ID verified, temporary badge issued and deactivated, and escorted when required), the count/rate of unauthorized visitor access incidents, and the timeliness of badge issuance/deactivation against policy targets.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring measurement of compliance/coverage (percent of visitor events meeting procedure). Counts and rates are useful secondary measures (unauthorized events, escort incidents) and trending shows ongoing enforcement.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of visitor arrivals per period with complete sign-in records (visitor name, company, host), ID verification flag, temporary badge issued (with activation and deactivation timestamps), and escort assignment when policy requires; count of physical access events tied to visitor badges that are labeled unauthorized or anomalous; mean time between badge activation and deactivation. Include CCTV correlation where available and records of policy exceptions.
data_signals: visitor sign-in logs (name, company, host, time-in, time-out)
ID verification records or scanned ID image references
temporary badge issuance records (badge ID, activation_timestamp, deactivation_timestamp)
escort assignment or host notification logs
physical access control events correlated to temporary badge IDs
records of unauthorized access incidents or 'breach' tagged physical incidents
CCTV footage indexes or retention logs for visitor zones
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated access control logs and badge activation/deactivation records correlated to visitor sign-ins (strong) vs. manual handwritten logbooks without timestamps (weak)
Continuous correlation between temporary badge events and access-control door events (strong) vs. occasional CCTV snapshots only (weak)
Consistent escort assignment records and host acknowledgements (strong) vs. ad-hoc verbal escorting with no logs (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: 'procedures enforced' indicates actions intended to prevent unauthorized physical access and breaches, aligning with a preventive control.

PES-3.differentiation_note: This control specifically targets physical visitor access enforcement (sign-in, ID verification, temporary badges, escorting) rather than equipment maintenance, disaster recovery, or remote-work security; note that an explicit 'physical security' focus area is not available in the provided list, so access_control/identity_and_access were chosen as the closest matches.

PES-4.measurement_goal: Measure coverage and operational effectiveness of environmental monitoring devices for all locations and assets that support critical systems: percentage of required locations with implemented and reporting devices, sensor uptime/heartbeat rate, and count/severity of environmental alerts and time-to-detect/respond against policy thresholds.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Primary focus is on coverage (what percentage of required areas/assets have implemented devices). Secondary metrics (counts of alerts, rate of heartbeats/alerts, and trends in alert frequency or uptime) evidence ongoing operation and detection capability.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of critical facilities/rooms/racks that have the required environmental monitoring devices deployed and registered in the device inventory. 2) Continuous telemetry indicators: sensor heartbeat/uplink events and sensor reading streams (timestamped), plus counts and severity of environmental alerts. 3) Time-based response metrics: time from alert to acknowledgment and time to remediate environmental incidents; maintenance/calibration records for devices.
data_signals: environmental device inventory records with device type and physical location mapped to critical assets
deployment/installation records showing device installed and commission timestamp
sensor telemetry streams (timestamped temperature/humidity/power/water/smoke readings) and last-seen heartbeat timestamps
device uptime/availability and percent uptime per device
environmental alert events with timestamps, severity, affected location, and alerting channel
incident/ticket records created from environmental alerts with timestamps for detection, acknowledgement, and remediation
device maintenance and calibration logs with dates and outcomes
device configuration and threshold settings showing required thresholds for alerting
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: continuous telemetry and automated alert logs tied to a maintained device inventory and mapped to critical assets (high-frequency heartbeat events, device uptime > policy threshold, automated alert -> ticket creation).
Weak: one-time photos or annual manual inspection reports without telemetry, or missing mapping between devices and critical asset inventory.
Strong: calibration/maintenance records aligned to device uptime and alert histories; Weak: undocumented or ad-hoc maintenance entries.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires environmental monitoring devices to be implemented, which is primarily preventive (reduces risk of equipment failure/unavailability) though it also provides detection; description emphasizes implementation.

PES-4.differentiation_note: This control focuses specifically on physical environmental device deployment and continuous telemetry coverage for locations supporting critical systems (coverage and uptime), whereas sibling controls in the domain may focus on access controls, backups, or recovery procedures; measurement must therefore emphasize device inventory-to-asset mapping and continuous sensor telemetry rather than purely access or configuration metrics.

PES-5.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of required environmental security inspections completed on schedule, the count and severity of inspection findings (equipment failures, environmental exceptions), and the remediation completion rate and mean time-to-remediate for inspection findings across facilities and critical infrastructure.
risk_categories: Access control, Physical and environmental security

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is primarily preventive (inspections and maintenance): auditors want coverage and completion rates (percentage). Counts of findings and trends over time are useful secondary measures to show frequency and remediation progress.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track scheduled vs completed environmental inspections per facility and per asset class, number of findings by category/severity per inspection, timestamps for when corrective actions were opened and closed, and facility availability or equipment-failure incident records tied to inspection findings. Aggregate by facility and compare against policy-required inspection frequency.
data_signals: inspection schedule records and completion timestamps
inspection checklist / findings records (finding type, severity)
corrective action / remediation ticket creation and closure timestamps
equipment failure incident logs tied to facility or asset
facility unavailability / outage incident reports
project plan items referencing environmental security tasks
records of assigned responsibilities for environmental/security tasks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated inspection scheduling and completion logs with timestamps, linked remediation tickets with closure timestamps and severity tags, and correlated incident logs showing reductions in equipment failures.
Weak: single point-in-time manual inspection memos without timestamps, undocumented corrective actions, or anecdotal statements that inspections occur.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name ('Environmental security inspected') indicates scheduled inspections and maintenance to prevent breaches and equipment failures, which is preventive in nature.

PES-5.differentiation_note: This control focuses on physical/environmental inspection coverage and remediation (schedules, checklists, equipment-failure correlation) rather than logical access or software configuration; because the valid focus_area list lacks an explicit 'physical_security' tag, it is mapped to cloud_and_infrastructure_security and access_control as the closest matches.

PRM-1.measurement_goal: Measure the existence, currency, and coverage of externally published company security and privacy commitments and the degree to which those commitments are linked to project plans and assigned responsibilities (e.g., percentage of externally published commitments that are current, approved, and referenced in active project plans).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Auditors need coverage and compliance ratios (percentage of commitments that are published, current, and linked to projects/responsibilities) as primary evidence; raw counts of published commitments and trends over time provide complementary detail on updates and remediation.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: The ideal metric is the percentage of externally published commitments (privacy/security statements, SLAs, SOC2 disclosures) that have an approved version, a publication/revision timestamp within policy-defined currency windows, and at least one linkage to an active project plan or a documented assigned responsibility. Also capture counts of published commitment items and time-since-last-update to show recency.
data_signals: external policy documents (privacy policy, security statement) with publication and revision timestamps
public SOC2/ISO/assurance report files and publication dates
customer-facing SLA and contract clause records referencing security commitments
website/page CMS change logs or publication timestamps for commitment pages
project plan documents or project requirement artifacts referencing the published commitment (document ID/link)
RACI/role-assignment records showing allocated responsibilities tied to commitment items
signed customer communications or contract acknowledgements referencing commitments
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: publicly accessible documents with verifiable publication/revision timestamps, version control history, and signed contract clauses linking commitments to customers or projects.
Strong: project plans and RACI artifacts that explicitly reference published commitment IDs and show assigned owners.
Weak: internal drafts, informal statements without timestamps, or screenshots without version history that do not link commitments to project artifacts or assigned responsibilities.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control focuses on publishing commitments externally to set expectations and allocate responsibilities, which acts to prevent misunderstandings and mitigate risks (preventive).

PRM-1.differentiation_note: Unlike sibling operational controls that focus on technical controls or incident detection, PRM-1 uniquely requires measurement of external-facing artifacts (published policies, SLAs, website content) and linkage to project plans and role assignments; because the control description is empty, keywords and measurement needs are inferred from associated risks.

PRM-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and availability of pre-arranged external support resources for remote work sites: percent of remote sites/endpoints covered by external support agreements, SLA compliance (response/resolution) for third‑party engagements, and count/rate of incidents where external support was engaged and the time-to-engage.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is about ensuring coverage and availability (a preventive assurance) so percentage metrics (coverage %, SLA compliance %) are primary; counts of engagements and rates/ trends of response times are useful secondary measures to show operational effectiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: 1) Percentage of documented remote work sites and endpoints that are covered by a signed external support contract or service arrangement. 2) SLA compliance rate for external support (response time and resolution time) over the measurement period. 3) Count of incidents requiring third‑party support and mean time to third‑party engagement (timestamp of incident -> timestamp of first vendor engagement).
data_signals: signed third‑party support contracts with coverage scope and SLAs
support contact directory / on‑call roster for external vendors
inventory of remote work sites and associated support coverage mapping
incident/ticket records with timestamps showing third‑party engagement
SLA response and resolution time logs for external vendors
endpoint inventory showing which endpoints have vendor support agents or enrollment
records of operational impact associated with insecure remote work sites (incident severity tags)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: signed contracts + automated SLA telemetry and incident tickets with third‑party engagement timestamps showing sustained SLA compliance over multiple periods.
Moderate: recent email confirmations and ad‑hoc engagement logs for incidents.
Weak: one‑time attestations or undocumented verbal agreements without timestamps or SLAs.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control name emphasizes that external support resources are 'available', indicating proactive arrangements to prevent or reduce impact of incidents at remote sites.

PRM-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically measures availability and coverage of external/third‑party support for remote work sites (contracts, contacts, SLAs and engagement timestamps), unlike sibling controls that might focus on internal configuration, detection, or remediation processes.

PRM-3.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and currency of published product and service descriptions: the percentage of all products/services that have a published description accessible to internal and external users, plus the count of descriptions published or updated within the policy-defined refresh period and evidence of distribution/access.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: As a preventive control, auditors need coverage/completeness measures (percentage of services with published descriptions). Counts and trends are useful secondary metrics to show the number of items and currency over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of cataloged products/services with a published description and accessible publication location (internal portal URL or external site), plus timestamps showing when each description was published or last updated and distribution/evidence of communication (access logs or acknowledgment receipts). Also capture service owner assignment and revision history to support currency and ownership.
data_signals: service_catalog entries (unique service/product identifiers)
service_description text field presence/length for each catalog entry
publication_timestamp / last_updated_timestamp for each description
publication_location (internal portal URL, external website URL) per service
access logs or view counts for description pages (IP/time/user)
distribution logs or acknowledgment receipts (email distribution, newsletter, or portal notification receipts)
service_owner assignment and document revision history entries
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: a maintained service catalog with programmatic publication timestamps, accessible URLs, service owner metadata, revision history, and distribution/acknowledgment logs showing delivery to internal/external audiences.
Weak: ad‑hoc screenshots or a single emailed PDF, missing timestamps, no accessible URLs, or descriptions that have not been updated in policy timeframe.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control states the company 'provides a description' to users, which is an action to prevent misunderstanding/misuse and is therefore preventive in nature.

PRM-3.differentiation_note: Unlike operational detection or remediation controls, this control focuses on documentation and communication coverage (publication, accessibility, and currency) rather than technical monitoring or incident response; measurement must emphasize catalog coverage, publication evidence, and distribution receipts.

RSK-1.measurement_goal: Demonstrate that formal risk assessment objectives are documented, approved, and mapped to key risk areas (including remote work/site risks and operational impact), and measure the percentage of required risk domains covered and recency of objective reviews.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This control is preventive (requires defined objectives and coverage). A percentage metric (coverage of required risk domains/objectives) directly shows completeness; counts and trends support inventory and recency; a score can summarize maturity.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count and percentage of documented risk assessment objectives required by policy that are present, the mapping of those objectives to identified risk areas (e.g., remote work sites, operational impact), timestamps of last review/approval, and the number of risk domains without coverage. Include the approved objectives document version and owner to allow a point-in-time verification.
data_signals: documented risk assessment objectives (document name, version, owner)
mapping table linking objectives to risk areas (e.g., 'remote work sites', 'operational impact')
policy requirement listing required risk domains and objective count
timestamp of last review/approval for objectives document
coverage percentage of required risk domains with at least one objective
count of uncovered or missing risk domains
evidence of approval (signature/approval record) and governance repository entry
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: versioned, approved objectives document stored in the governance repository with explicit mapping to risk areas (including 'remote work sites' and 'operational impact') and a recent review timestamp.
Weak: ad-hoc emails, informal notes, or stakeholder statements without a versioned document, no mapping to specific risks, or no approval record.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires specification of objectives (establishing requirements before incidents), which is a preventive governance activity.

RSK-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on the specification and documented coverage of risk assessment objectives (policy/charter existence and mapping to risks like remote work), not on execution of assessments or remediation metrics; it therefore requires document- and coverage-oriented measurements rather than detection or remediation counts.

RSK-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and recency of documented information security risk assessments across projects, remote-work contexts, and mobile device use; and measure the percent of identified risks that have assigned treatment plans with target completion dates and evidence of remediation tracking.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is fundamentally a preventive coverage control — auditors need coverage and completion rates (percent of projects assessed, percent of risks treated). Counts and trending are useful complements to show volume and improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Percentage of active projects with a documented information security risk assessment performed within the required policy timeframe; count and percent of risk entries (including mobile device and remote-work related risks) that have an assigned treatment plan, owner, and target/completion timestamps; trend in assessment coverage and percent-of-risks-treated over recent reporting periods.
data_signals: risk assessment record or report identifiers with creation/last-review timestamps
project registry entries linking to risk assessment IDs (project plans referencing risk assessments)
risk register rows tagged with scopes: 'mobile device', 'remote work', or project ID
treatment plan entries with owner, target remediation date, and completion timestamp
coverage metric: count of assessed projects vs. total active projects
last assessment date per asset/project and days-since-last-assessment
open/unresolved risk count and overdue remediation count
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between project records and risk assessment documents, up-to-date risk register entries with remediation timestamps and owners, and periodic assessment completion logs demonstrating consistent coverage.
Weak: one-off or ad-hoc PDF risk reports without project linkage, missing remediation due dates, or undocumented treatment ownership.

affinity_keywords: compromise of company systems and data

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control requires performing risk assessments and establishing treatments (preventing unassessed risks), implied by the name 'Risks assessments performed' and associated risk descriptions about risks not being identified or treated.

RSK-2.differentiation_note: This entry focuses specifically on coverage, recency, and treatment-tracking of risk assessments across projects, remote-work, and mobile-device contexts — its unique measurement need is verifying linkage between project plans and risk assessment records and tracking treatment completion rather than technical vulnerability scanning or incident detection.

RSK-3.measurement_goal: Measure organization-wide coverage and operational effectiveness of the risk management program: percentage of active projects with documented security risk assessments, count and severity distribution of risks logged, percentage of risks with assigned owners and remediation plans, and resolution rate/timeliness for identified segregation-of-duties conflicts and mobile/remote-work related risks.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is a preventive, program-level control where auditors need coverage and compliance rates (e.g., percent of projects with risk assessments, percent of risks with owners). Counts (number of open risks) and trends (remediation pace over time) supplement coverage metrics; a maturity score can summarize program health.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute: (a) percent of active projects that include a documented information-security risk assessment or security section in the project plan; (b) counts of risks logged into the risk register by category (remote work, mobile devices, SoD, project plans) and their severity; (c) percent of logged risks with an assigned owner and active mitigation plan; (d) remediation/closure rate and mean time-to-treat for risks, and counts of unresolved segregation-of-duties conflicts.
data_signals: project plan records/documents indicating presence or absence of security risk assessment
risk register entries (risk id, description, category/tag such as 'remote work' or 'mobile device', severity)
risk owner assignment and mitigation plan fields (action items, due dates)
risk treatment action logs and completion timestamps
mobile device inventory and associated risk assessment records
remote-work site security assessment reports
segregation-of-duties/conflict-of-interest exception logs and access assignment records
operational impact or incident reports referencing identified risks
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped risk register entries linked to project records and ticketed mitigation tasks with owners and completion timestamps; periodic program maturity scores and trending remediation metrics.
Weak: one-off audit memos, undated spreadsheets, or anecdotal statements that do not link risks to projects, owners, or tracked remediation actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control establishes a risk management program intended to identify and treat risks (prevention), as implied by the name and associated risks about risks not being identified or treated.

RSK-3.differentiation_note: Unlike technical detection or remediation controls, RSK-3 requires program-level coverage and process metrics across projects, remote/mobile endpoints, and SoD — measurement must link project plans, the enterprise risk register, and mitigation action tracking rather than only technical scan outputs.

SAT-1.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and timeliness of required security awareness training: % of employees assigned the mandated training by role who completed it within the required timeframe, counts of overdue or non‑compliant employees, and training acknowledgement records to demonstrate implementation and accountability.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Training controls are preventive and coverage/compliance percentages (completion rate, coverage by role) best show whether the control is implemented; counts and rates (overdue counts, completion velocity) are useful secondary measures and trends show change over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Compute the percentage of employees (and by role/department) who were assigned required security awareness training and completed it within the policy timeframe; include timestamps of assignment and completion, the training content/version, and counts of overdue or unassigned personnel. Also capture acknowledgement receipts and any remediation actions for non‑compliance.
data_signals: training assignment records (user, training_id, role, assigned_timestamp)
training completion records (user, training_id, completion_timestamp, score if applicable)
employee roster with role/department and employment status
training content/version ID and effective date
acknowledgement/attestation records for policy receipt
overdue training counts and remediation action logs
periodic compliance reports (completion % by role/department, by period)
evidence_strength_indicators: Automated LMS logs showing assignment and completion timestamps (strong) versus one-off manual attestations or undated certificates (weak)
High coverage by role and low overdue counts with documented remediation actions (strong) versus sporadic completion snapshots without role mapping (weak)
Versioned training content and evidence that latest required module was delivered (strong) versus undated/unnamed training artifacts (weak)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control title states 'Security awareness training implemented', which is a preventive activity intended to reduce security incidents by educating staff.

SAT-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on training coverage, timeliness, and acknowledgement records (people/process metrics) rather than technical controls or incident detection — measurement must map to LMS assignment/completion data and role-based coverage. If broader controls reference policy or project planning, this entry is specific to training implementation evidence.

TPM-1.measurement_goal: Measure the percentage of third‑party vendors with executed contracts that include required security clauses (e.g., breach notification, security controls, segregation of duties), broken down by vendor criticality, and track timeliness of contract review/renewal and remediation of missing clauses.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control requiring coverage of contracts across the vendor population (coverage % is the most direct proof). Counts of missing/overdue agreements and trends over time are useful secondary signals to show remediation and review cadence.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of vendors in the official vendor inventory and the count/percentage that have an executed agreement (signed timestamp) with clause-level metadata (breach notification, security requirements, segregation of duties). Include vendor risk_tier, last_contract_review_date, agreement_expiration_date, and number of vendor accounts or access entitlements tied to each vendor to show scope and enforcement.
data_signals: vendor inventory rows (vendor_id, name, risk_tier)
contract repository records with executed_date and signer_id
contract clause checklist flags (breach_notification, security_controls, segregation_of_duties)
contract last_review_date and expiration_date
access provisioning records showing vendor accounts and entitlements (vendor_id → resource access)
exceptions or approved deviations log for vendors lacking clauses
evidence of breach notification procedure in contract (clause text or contract template id)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated linkage between vendor inventory and contract repository showing executed_date and clause-level flags for all vendors, plus periodic review timestamps and remediation tickets for missing clauses.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or single sample contracts without clause metadata, or manual attestations without signed timestamps.
Strong: proof that vendor access is provisioned only when contract clause flags are present; weak: contracts present but no control linking to access or enforcement actions.

control_type_classification.reasoning: Requiring established third‑party agreements is a preventive control that aims to prevent vendor-caused breaches by specifying obligations (e.g., breach notification, security controls).

TPM-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on contractual coverage and clause-level completeness for vendors (document evidence and linkage to vendor inventory), unlike sibling controls that might measure technical access controls or incident response for vendors; therefore measurements must include contract metadata and linkage to vendor access and risk tier.

TPM-2.measurement_goal: Measure the coverage and operational effectiveness of the vendor management program by reporting vendor inventory completeness, percent of vendors with documented security assessments/contracts/DPA, count and rate of vendor-related security incidents, and timeliness of vendor remediation and review activities.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective controls benefit from counts (e.g., number of vendor incidents, assessments completed) to show detection activity; percentages show coverage (assessment/contract coverage) and trends show program improvement over time.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Concrete metrics: total vendor count and vendor inventory completeness; count and percent of vendors with completed security assessments (type and date); count and percent of vendors with signed Data Processing Agreements or security clauses; count of vendor-originated security incidents and time-to-remediate vendor findings; frequency (dates) of periodic vendor risk reviews and assigned vendor owners.
data_signals: vendor inventory records (vendor_id, service, criticality, assigned_owner)
security assessment status per vendor (assessment_type e.g., SOC2/ISO/pen test, assessment_date, result)
contract metadata flags (DPA_present, security_clause_present, contract_effective_date)
vendor incident reports (incident_id, vendor_id, date_reported, severity, impact 'breach' flag)
vendor remediation task items (finding_id, vendor_id, created_date, resolved_date, resolution_status)
vendor risk review logs (review_date, reviewer, outcome and next_review_date)
onboarding/offboarding checklist records for vendors (completed_steps, timestamps)
assigned responsibilities and approval records for vendor onboarding (approver_id, approval_timestamp, segregation_of_duties evidence)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: centralized vendor inventory and automated records showing assessment dates, signed DPA/contract artifacts, and time-stamped incident reports tied to vendor IDs; automated alerts or logs for vendor-originated breaches and tracked remediation items.
Weak: ad-hoc spreadsheets or isolated email threads claiming vendor reviews, or single annual attestations without timestamps tying to active vendor inventory.

control_type_classification.reasoning: The control is described as a 'vendor management program' and was provided as a detective control; measurement focuses on detecting vendor issues (e.g., breaches, missing assessments) rather than preventive configuration.

TPM-2.differentiation_note: Unlike asset-level controls, this control requires program-level vendor coverage and per-vendor evidence (assessments, contracts, incident linkage and owner assignments) rather than technical configuration or patching metrics.

VPM-1.measurement_goal: Measure the scope and timeliness of maintenance actions on service infrastructure: percentage of infrastructure assets with current baseline configurations and successful maintenance (patching/backups) within policy windows, plus counts of overdue or unmaintained assets and related change tickets to demonstrate ongoing maintenance.

metric_type_preferences.rationale: This is primarily a preventive control (maintaining infrastructure), so percentage metrics (coverage/compliance rates) show whether policy thresholds are met; counts and trends (e.g., overdue assets, patch volume over time) provide supporting operational context.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Track asset-level maintenance status and actions: asset inventory completeness with owner/contact, last maintenance/patch date, configuration compliance scan results, vulnerability scan findings by asset, backup job success/failure, and change ticket records for maintenance windows. Calculate percent of assets meeting the maintenance policy (baseline config, patched within policy window, recent successful backup) and count of assets overdue beyond policy thresholds.
data_signals: CMDB/asset inventory records with fields: asset_id, service_infrastructure_tag, owner_contact, last_maintenance_date
patch deployment logs/timestamps per asset (patch_id, applied_timestamp, success/failure)
configuration compliance scan results with rule IDs and pass/fail per asset
vulnerability scan results per asset (CVE identifiers, CVSS score, detection_timestamp)
backup job logs (job_id, asset_id, start_time, end_time, status)
change management tickets for maintenance (ticket_id, asset_id, scheduled_window, completed_timestamp, status)
count of assets with last_maintenance_date older than policy_threshold
incident/availability logs related to infrastructure (outage events, resolution time)
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated, timestamped CMDB and patch/backup logs showing continuous coverage and recent successful maintenance for > policy_threshold% of assets
Strong: change tickets linked to asset records with completed timestamps within scheduled maintenance windows
Weak: occasional manual checklists or one-off snapshots without timestamps or asset linkage
Weak: policy documents alone without machine-readable evidence (CMDB, logs, scan outputs)

control_type_classification.reasoning: The phrase 'maintained' implies ongoing preventative activities (patching/configuration/backups) to avoid incidents rather than detection or remediation.

VPM-1.differentiation_note: This control focuses on operational evidence of maintenance (patching, configuration compliance, backups, CMDB completeness) rather than incident contacts, threat intelligence, or project-level security planning referenced in associated risks.

VPM-2.measurement_goal: Measure the quarterly scan coverage of all external-facing hosts (percentage and count), the count of critical/high vulnerabilities found per scan cycle, and remediation effectiveness (remediation completion rate and mean time-to-remediate for critical/high findings).

metric_type_preferences.rationale: Detective control: primary event counts (scan runs, vulnerabilities found, open vs closed) demonstrate detection activity; percentage (coverage) and rate (remediation / SLA adherence) supplement counts to show completeness and responsiveness.

evidence_requirements.what_to_measure: Count of host-based scan executions per external-facing host per quarter; percent of external-facing host inventory scanned at least once within the quarter; open and closed vulnerability records filtered by severity (critical, high) with timestamps for detection and remediation; remediation ticket status and closure timestamps to calculate remediation rate and MTTR for critical/high findings.
data_signals: host-based vulnerability scan execution logs with timestamps and host IDs
asset inventory records with external-facing flag / network exposure attribute
scan coverage ratio: number of external-facing hosts scanned / total external-facing hosts
vulnerability findings table with severity (critical/high), CVE/CVSS identifiers, detection timestamp
remediation ticket or change record with remediation start and closure timestamps
open vulnerability count by severity at quarter boundaries
overdue remediation list (critical/high past remediation target)
scan failure/error logs or scan miss reasons
evidence_strength_indicators: Strong: automated scheduled scan logs tied to canonical asset inventory showing every external-facing host scanned each quarter, with linked vulnerability records and remediation ticket timestamps.
Strong: demonstrable remediation workflow entries (tickets/changes) closing critical/high findings with timestamps and verification evidence.
Weak: one-off quarterly summary reports or screenshots without raw scan logs, or spreadsheets that lack asset mapping to external-facing hosts.
Weak: vulnerability counts without remediation timestamps or without linkage to an authoritative asset inventory (cannot prove coverage).

control_type_classification.reasoning: The description states 'Host-based vulnerability scans are performed at least quarterly' and indicates detection and tracking of vulnerabilities, classifying it as a detective control.

VPM-2.differentiation_note: This control specifically requires host-based scans of external-facing systems on a quarterly cadence and tracking of critical/high findings to remediation — unlike sibling controls that might focus on patch deployment or application scanning, this one centers on scan coverage of external-facing hosts and remediation tracking.
